weren't able to do this if it hadn't been for a community so we actually reached out to the association that was based in Mariana that has been working in Mariana for 36 years Atacama Atacama had the system and surveys in order to identify the needs of the people and distribute and deliver things according to what they needed where they needed it and it was through this process of doing that we were able to move from a place of despair to beginning to heal the way would work is that the kids would arrive at 9:30 in the morning they would set up the tables and chairs and then they would start playing chess and pretty soon hundreds of people would arrive and then we would deliver food to about 60 people across the mountain in the community and then everyone around us was asking wanting to join and we asked them how contribute whatever you know how to do what do you love to do come and join us and there was this one young man who approached us and he said you're just regular people what really help anymore not without an agenda and then he started seeing what we were doing and he decided to not leave Puerto Rico and join the military and instead he stayed and he studied on the island because he started to see that a different word that Rico was possible and this is when we took a step back and looked at our work and we realized we are building a different Porto Rico we're building it because you see when the entire physical and material infrastructure collapsed the only thing that remained was the invisible life-saving infrastructure which is our knowledge of where we are in relation to each other our knowledge of each other's skills our capacity to work with our neighbor and sometimes forgive our neighbor and mobilize because when disasters happen the person right in front of you is your best chance at survival and this is when we understood that the times we will be facing as humanity will require us to recognize that community is the most important thing around us because when there's no ATMs no communication systems no food no water nothing you need to be able to tap into a pre-existing system of respect of trust of reciprocity and from there you can rebuild in Mariana we knew that we needed communication systems we needed solar power we needed water systems we spent six months without running water nine months without electricity so when we put out the call for communication systems a donor answered and she funded a group of former US Coast Guard errs who responded to Sandy and within 24 hours they came to Mariana and they installed Wi-Fi using solar powered radios so we looked around and we said well why can't we have Wi-Fi and Mariana for everyone why can't we have our own radio system and it we realized that it was all we had to do was listen to the people in front of us the people on the ground they knew they were the experts in what they needed so when people started coming to grab a meal with a hands raw purplish from hand washing clothes in the river we realize we need to build a solar laundromat and we did and then we realized we need a micro grid a solar micro grid and we got a solar micro grid and filters and an emergency plan pretty soon we were building a model of what a community needs to have to be healthy enough and organized enough in order to be able to face a natural disaster and our vision now is to be able to share markers of how we know if communities are ready to be able to respond to extreme weather conditions because it is communities like mine these so called marginalized communities they have had to create alternative systems of survival because they have had a lack a systemic lack of basic human right services but if well invested in and well organized these communities are better suited to respond when disasters happen and we owe it to millions of communities like ours around the world to share these lessons and help them prepare for disasters we owe it to the future generations that will face a disproportionate burden of climate change we owe it to the ancestors and the activists and leaders that have gotten us this far and we owe it to the over three in Puerto turns out communities in Porto Rico are powerful beyond measure they may not make the headlines but they are generous they're collaborative they're gonna come we're gonna come up with some of the most I'm not gonna say I wasn't afraid, but there was no way I was going to abandon ship. - I got a text message from one of the tenants who I organized with. They put this notice on our door. She sent me a picture of it that said, just a reminder, October rent is still due. I was livid because in that same text message, she had said, you know, we don't have any food. We don't have any water, and I was like, we have to do something. People cannot pay rent for October, especially right now because all the jobs are gone. - I'm on this tiny little hill, which my basement flooded, but ultimately everything was okay, and so somebody dropped water off at the base of my driveway, and then somebody else wanted to drop off a little bit of food and somebody else wanted to drop off a little bit of clothing, and I ended up getting some tarps out and going that direction. It just wasn't an option in my head. I had the resources, I knew what to do. - Corrine, Jen and Katie rolled up their sleeves and reached out, helping in ways only they could. - Many people on my staff sent their families away to safer places because that's what they felt was needed, and they stayed behind to make sure that that the election ran because that is how important it is to them and how important they think it is to other people. We had people knocking on our doors shortly after on their way out of evacuating asking us would the election go on, and because of that dedication, that heart that my staff has, I could confidently tell them yes. - We got the forms from the housing authority and made copies and went with a team and knocked on every single door and every single housing property, and gave people the hardship waivers, - And it just exploded into three event tents. One of them looks like a grocery store, the other one looks like a clothing department store, and then there's one for storage, and I had two storage units as well as my garage serving a few hundred people. - Their efforts didn't just heal, they transformed lives and uncovered something extraordinary. - It's really was important for me to find that community and to understand these systems, to realize that I have a voice and that I can use it. And so I've seen what happens with other people, especially since the storm who have found their voice and have stood up for their community's needs, and they are just, - It's life changing. We demonstrated that when it gets tough, we focus on supporting each other and those differences in opinion and philosophy and political affiliation, they all fall away. None of that divisiveness matters, and we just come together. We make a plan and we help each other. That is what we did, and we do that in every crisis. To me, that's really the spirit of America.

  3. @mk202 [3 likes]: My father had a commercial fishing business in Gloucester during the 80’s. We had migrated back to MA from where we were living in Chicago because my father’s lifelong dream was to have fishing boats working out of Gloucester. 

We lived in Magnolia and he had two 90 foot trawlers that docked in Rose’s Wharf. It was the happiest time of his life, and he was very successful for many years. My comment is more about Gloucester itself, though. 

The people in Gloucester form the most hard working, family oriented, resilient, community I have ever known. Gloucester is one of  the very few places you’ll find that has never forgotten the past, and the spirit you see in this film from 60 years ago is remarkably well preserved to this day. 

It’s 19 years old now, but the film The Perfect Storm is an excellent snapshot of Gloucester and it’s fascinating culture in more modern times. It’s a great story, and the depiction of the local culture of the fishing industry on both water and land in the early 1990’s is represented with great accuracy and depth. 

If Gloucester interests you- it’s a must see. 😉

issues is irresponsible they cite concerns about government spending and the national debt these lawmakers believe that fiscal responsibility should not be overlooked even in the face of natural hampered by the lack of resources the politicization of disaster relief undermines FEMA's ability to effectively serve those in need the agency's mission is to help communities recover and rebuild but political interference can make this Mission difficult to achieve it underscores the need for a more compassionate and bipartisan approach to disaster funding by setting aside political differences Bankruptcy filings are rising month over month, hitting everyone from massive corporations to local mom and pop businesses and even the farming industry, indicative of the recent farmer bailout, which is a replay of the first Trump administration. Consumer bankruptcies have also risen steadily each month. The math is simple. When tariffs add on average 1,200 to the household budget, they're only three ways out. You take on more debt. Michael Johnson, a 42-year-old American living in Queens, New York, was not homeless. He had a home and a job, but the power went out that night. Heater off, house as cold as ice. Michael thought he would sit in the car and turn on the heater for a while. This decision became the last decision of his life. It was snowing outside. The trees were falling. The roads were frozen. This was America's biggest travel stoppage after Corona. Snow in the north , darkness in the south, 2 feet of snow had accumulated in the North East. Snowfall in the South , electric poles broken. This Hurricane Fern wasn't just a weather event. This was a warning. Remember, no one can stand against nature. No matter which corner of the world you are from. - What If I Don't Renew My Policy? - Auto Coverage Explained: Kind: captions Language: en What if I don't renew my policy? What happens if you decide not to renew your car insurance policy? This is a question that many drivers may not fully consider, but it is essential to understand the potential consequences. First and foremost, if you do not renew your policy, you will not have any coverage for accidents or damages that occur after your policy term ends. This means that if you are involved in an accident, you will be responsible for all costs, including property damage, medical expenses, and any legal claims from others. These costs can add up quickly and may lead to serious financial difficulties. Additionally, driving without insurance is illegal in most states. If your policy lapses, the motor vehicle department may be notified. This could result in penalties such as fines or even the suspension of your driver's license. In some cases, you may also be required to file a certificate of financial responsibility, which can be an expensive requirement and may lead to higher insurance premiums in the future. Another important point to consider is that even a brief lapse in coverage can lead to increased insurance rates when you seek a new policy. Insurance companies often view continuous coverage as a sign of responsible driving. Therefore, if there is a gap in your coverage, it may result in higher premiums when you apply for a new policy. In many cases, starting a new policy after a lapse can be more expensive than simply renewing your existing one. If your vehicle is financed or leased, not maintaining insurance could violate the terms of your loan or lease agreement. This could lead to your vehicle being repossessed by the lender or leasing company, which is a situation no one wants to face. It is also important to differentiate between not renewing your policy and having your policy cancelled midterm. Non-renewal occurs at the end of the policy term and typically requires you to find new coverage. However, midterm cancellations usually happen due to serious issues like non-payment or fraud and can carry more severe penalties. In summary, choosing not to renew your car insurance policy can leave you without coverage, expose you to legal penalties, increase your financial risks, and potentially lead to higher future insurance costs. To avoid these negative outcomes, it is wise to renew your policy on time or secure new coverage before your current policy expires.

- How Do Auto Insurance Cancellation And Non-renewal Differ? - Auto Coverage Explained: Kind: captions Language: en [Applause] [Music] How do auto insurance cancellation and non-renewal differ? Imagine you're driving along and suddenly your insurance coverage disappears. It might be because your policy was cancelled or because it simply wasn't renewed. These two situations might seem similar, but they are quite different. Let's explore what sets them apart so you can manage your auto insurance better. Auto insurance cancellation happens when your insurer ends your coverage before the policy scheduled end date. This can happen for reasons like missed payments, fraud, or if your driver's license gets suspended. Usually, the insurer must give you a notice, often about 10 days before cancelling. If your policy gets cancelled, you are immediately without coverage, which can be risky if you get into an accident. On the other hand, non-renewal occurs when your insurer decides not to continue your coverage once your current policy expires. This is a decision made at the end of your policy term, which is typically 1 year. Insurers usually give you a longer notice, often around 60 days, so you have time to find a new policy. Non-renewal is common if you have a poor driving record, multiple claims, or if the insurer is changing its business focus. The reasons behind cancellation are often urgent or serious like unpaid premiums or license issues. Non-renewal might be due to broader reasons such as changes in risk profile or the insurer pulling out of certain areas. If your policy is canled, you lose coverage immediately, which can leave you unprotected. But with non-renewal, you get advanced notice, giving you time to shop for a new policy before your current one ends. It's also good to know that you can usually cancel your own policy whenever you want. But if your insurer cancels or non-renews you, they need to provide reasons if you ask. You might even have the right to appeal or file a complaint if you think the decision was unfair. When managing your auto insurance, understanding these differences helps you stay prepared. If you get a cancellation notice, act quickly to find new coverage. If you see a non-renewal coming, use the extra time to compare policies and secure a new one before your current coverage stops. In short, cancellation is an early ending of your policy, often for serious reasons. While non-renewal is a decision not to continue coverage at the end of your policy period, usually with more notice. Both situations requiring your attention to avoid gaps in your protection, but they happen at different times and for different reasons.

- EVERYTHING GONE IN A DAY: 15-Year Journey to Rebuild After Hurricane Katrina | A True Survival Story: Kind: captions Language: en through the storm. Rebuilding after Hurricane Katrina. I never expected to lose everything at 53. When you reach that age, you think you've weathered life storms. You've built something stable. A home, a family, memories contained in photo albums and trinkets collected over decades. But on August 29th, 2005, I learned how quickly it could all be washed away. My name is Marcus Johnson. For 30 years, I taught music at a high school in New Orleans Lower 9th Ward. My wife, Darlene, and I raised three children in our modest twostory home on Jerdan Avenue, just a few blocks from the industrial canal. We were part of a tight-knit community, the kind where neighbors watched out for each other, and Saturday cookouts spilled from one yard to the next. We'd seen hurricanes before. Growing up in New Orleans means learning to live with the threat of storms. We'd evacuated a few times over the years, packed the car with clothes and photo albums, secured the house as best we could, and driven to stay with Darlene's sister in Houston until the danger passed. Most times we'd returned to find minor damage. A fence down, shingles torn from the roof, water in the garage, nothing we couldn't handle. When the warnings about Katrina began, we initially planned to do the same, but Darlene's mother had been battling pneumonia in Turo Infirmary, and my wife was reluctant to leave her. "It'll blow over like they always do," she said, though the weather reports were more ominous than usual. By the time we realized the severity of the approaching storm, the highways were clogged with evacuees. Mayor Negan had ordered a mandatory evacuation, but thousands remained in the city. Those without transportation, the elderly, the stubborn, and yes, people like us, torn by difficult choices. We made a lastm minute decision to split up. Darlene would stay with her mother at the hospital, which had backup generators and was on higher ground. I would remain at home with our dog Charlie, a golden retriever who'd been part of our family for 11 years. Our children were grown and living in different states, safe at least from what was coming. The night before Katrina hit, I called each of my kids. I love you, I told them, trying to keep my voice steady. I'll call as soon as it's over. I didn't know it would be weeks before they heard my voice again. The storm itself was terrifying. Howling winds that sounded like freight trains, rain so heavy it seemed to be coming from all directions. I'd moved to the second floor with Charlie, some food, water, and a batterypowered radio. Through the night and into the morning, I listened to the destruction, the cracking of trees, the crash of objects hitting the house. But it was what came after that changed everything. I was dozing fitfully when I heard a sound like thunder followed by a rush of water. The industrial canal levy had breached. Within minutes, water was pouring into our neighborhood. I looked out the window to see it rising rapidly, cars already submerged, front porches disappearing. I grabbed Charlie and a few essentials and made it to the attic just as water began flowing up the stairs. The hatch to the roof was stuck. I had to kick it open. We emerged into eerie daylight. The neighborhood transformed into a vast lake with rooftops like islands. I won't dwell on those days on the roof. The blistering sun. The night so dark you couldn't see your hand in front of your face. The helicopters passing overhead. The cries from other rooftops. Charlie pressed against me as confused and frightened as I was. On the third day, a Coast Guard helicopter spotted us. The rescue swimmer who came down told me I could only bring what I could carry in one hand. I had to leave almost everything behind. I looked at Charlie, then at the rescuer. The dog comes, I said. It wasn't a question. The young man nodded. We'll make it work. We were dropped at an emergency collection point on the interstate. From there, buses took us to the Superdome, which had become a shelter of last resort. The scenes there haunt me still. Thousands of people in deteriorating conditions, inadequate food and water, overflowing toilets, the heat and smell unbearable. I kept Charlie close, sharing my mega rations with him, both of us shell shocked. Through it all, I worried constantly about Darlene. The hospital had been evacuated, I learned. But to where? Communications were down throughout the city. The not knowing was its own kind of torture. After four nightmarish days in the Superdome, I was evacuated to a shelter in Baton Rouge. It was there, scrolling through handwritten lists of evacuees posted on walls that I saw Darlene's name. She'd been taken to another shelter in the same city. Our reunion the next day was one of those moments where joy and grief collide. We held each other and cried for finding each other, for what we'd lost. For her mother, who had died during the hospital evacuation, for our ruined home and community, I tried to save the photo albums, I told her, breaking down. Those irreplaceable memories, our wedding, the children's birthdays, family holidays, all gone. We're alive, she said, cupping my face in her hands. We start from there. Over the following weeks, we moved between shelters, then to a FEMA trailer. We registered for assistance, stood in endless lines, filled out countless forms. Charlie became a favorite among the children in the shelters, a small bright spot in their disrupted lives. 2 months after Katrina, we were able to return briefly to our neighborhood. Nothing prepared us for what we found. Our house was still standing, but barely. The waterline reached almost to the second floor. Inside was unrecognizable. Furniture overturned, walls bloated with mold, everything coated in dried muck. The piano where I taught countless lessons to neighborhood kids was warped beyond repair. The family photos I'd tried to save dissolved into unrecognizable pulp. We salvaged almost nothing. Just a few water-damaged momentos and my father's pocket watch that had somehow survived in a dresser drawer. Our neighbors homes were in similar or worse condition. Some houses had been completely washed away, leaving only concrete slabs. The streets were deserted except for military vehicles and the occasional cleanup crew. "We can't come back here," Darlene said, her voice hollow. "There's nothing left to come back to." She was right. The lower ninth ward, once vibrant with life and music, had become a ghost town. basic services, electricity, water, gas, would take months or years to restore. The schools where we'd worked were destroyed. And like many in our community, our insurance wouldn't cover most of the damage. The fine print excluding flood damage had sealed our fate. We faced the hardest decision of our lives, whether to rebuild or relocate. Darlene's sister offered us a place to stay in Houston while we figured things out. With few option, we accepted. Those first months in Houston were a blur of grief, paperwork, and uncertainty. I found work as a substitute music teacher, while Darlene took a job at a department store. We lived in Rachel's spare bedroom. Charlie adapting to apartment life. Every day brought a new humiliation. borrowing clothes, asking for rides, learning to live with constant charity. Our children wanted us to move near them. New York, Chicago, Atlanta. Start fresh, they said. But something in me resisted. New Orleans wasn't just where we lived. It was part of who we were. I can't explain it, I told Darlene one night. But I feel like if we leave for good, we're giving up on more than just our house. She nodded, understanding. I feel it, too. 6 months after Katrina, we returned to New Orleans to make a final decision. The city was still reeling, but signs of recovery had begun to emerge. Some businesses had reopened. Residents were trickling back. Volunteers from around the country were helping with clean up and rebuilding. Our neighborhood remained largely abandoned, but we noticed small changes. A cleaned up lot here, a FEMA trailer there, a newly planted tree. We met Mr. Wilson, our elderly neighbor, who'd refused to leave. He was living in a small trailer beside his ruined home, determined to rebuild. "This is home," he said simply. "Where else would I go?" His words resonated with us. That evening, sitting in a small rented room, Darlene and I made our decision. We would return, but not to the lower 9inth ward, at least not yet. We would start smaller in a part of the city that had seen less damage, where recovery was further along. With our insurance settlement, inadequate as it was, FEMA assistance, and a loan against my pension, we bought a small duplex in Gentilly. The previous owners had evacuated and decided not to return. The house needed work, new drywall, floors, appliances, but the structure was sound. We moved back to New Orleans in June 2006, almost a year after Katrina. Our new home wasn't in our old neighborhood. Didn't hold our history. But it was a start. Charlie seemed especially pleased to have a yard again. Remaking our lives took time and presented challenges we'd never imagined. Simple tasks, finding a doctor, getting mail delivered, registering a car, had become complicated in post Katrina New Orleans. Services we took for granted before. Reliable electricity, garbage collection, police response were inconsistent at best. I found a teaching position at a charter school that had reopened. The school was overcrowded, underequipped and housed in a temporary building. But the students, many traumatized by their own Katrina experiences, needed stability and normaly. Music gave them that. In those first months back, I saw how healing it could be to create something beautiful amid so much destruction. Darlene, always more adaptable than me, reinvented herself completely. Before Katrina, she'd worked as an administrative assistant at the same school where I taught. Now, inspired by her experience with displacement, she took a position with a nonprofit helping evacuees navigate the Byzantine process of securing assistance and rebuilding. I lived it, she would tell her clients. I know how overwhelming this is. Our new life developed its own rhythm. We made new friends among our neighbors and co-workers, many of whom shared similar stories of loss and reinvention. On weekends, we often drove to the lower 9inth ward to check on reconstruction efforts and visit Mr. Wilson, who was slowly rebuilding his house with help from church volunteers. We also began the painstaking process of replacing what was lost. New clothes to fill our closets, kitchen items, furniture, some secondhand, some donated. But the hardest to replace were the personal items, the photographs, letters, children's artwork, family heirlooms. Those were simply gone. Or so we thought. One Saturday about 18 months after Katrina, we received a call from a woman named Grace. She'd found a waterlogged photo album washed up in her yard in Arab, several miles from our old home. Inside was a family photo with our names written on the back. "I've been trying to find you for months," she said. "I saved what I could." When we met her, she handed over a plastic container with our salvaged album. Most of the photos were ruined, but miraculously, a few had survived. Our wedding portrait, a Christmas with the kids when they were small, a faded image of me as a young man with my first school band. I knew these would be important to someone, Grace said. After losing so much myself, I understand the value of memories. That gesture of kindness from a stranger touched us deeply. It reminded us that even in the worst circumstances, humanity persists. Grace became a friend, one of many connections formed in the aftermath of tragedy. As months turned into years, our city slowly healed. Not completely. New Orleans would never be exactly what it was before. Some neighborhoods, including parts of the Lower 9inth Ward, remained sparsely populated. Many former residents never returned, creating a diaspora of New Orleanians across the country. The city's demographics and character shifted in subtle and not so subtle ways. But the resilience of those who stayed or returned was remarkable. Restaurants reopened with limited menus, but the same devotion to local cuisine. Music clubs brought back live performances. Mardigra parades resumed, perhaps more cathartic than celebratory at first. In 2009, 4 years after Katrina, I retired from teaching. The charter school had stabilized, finding a permanent location and expanding its music program. I felt I could step back, making way for younger teachers with fresh energy. Darlene continued her work with the nonprofit, which had expanded to help with broader community revitalization efforts. With more time on my hands, I began volunteering, teaching music to children in still struggling neighborhoods. One afternoon each week, I'd set up in a community center in the lower 9inth ward, offering free lessons to anyone interested. Sometimes only a few children would show up. Other times, I'd have a dozen eager faces before me. It was during one of these sessions that I met Deshaawn, a solemn 10-year-old with an intuitive understanding of rhythm. He rarely spoke, but transformed when he played the drums I'd brought. His grandmother, Miss Loretta, would watch from the sidelines, [Music] beaming. He's had a hard time since the storm, she told me after one lesson. Barely talks, but this this is bringing him back. Deshaawn reminded me why music had always been my calling. It wasn't just about notes and technique. It was about expression, connection, healing. In our broken city, making music together became an act of reclamation. As the years passed, our lives found a new normal. We adjusted to our smaller home, our altered routines, our evolved city. Charlie grew old and eventually passed peacefully. At the age of 17, having experienced both the worst and best of humanity, we adopted another dog, a scrappy rescue named Duke, who never knew the pre-Katrina world. We marked anniversaries 5 years since the storm. Then 10. Each August 29th, the city would collectively remember with memorial services and community events. For us, these anniversaries were complicated. Solemn recognition of all that was lost, but also acknowledgment of survival and renewal. 15 years after Katrina in 2020, another crisis emerged. The coid9 pandemic. As our city once again faced disaster, I noticed something remarkable. The resilience developed after Katrina served New Orleans well. People checked on neighbors, supported local businesses, found creative ways to maintain community despite isolation. The lessons of one catastrophe helped navigate another. Now approaching my 70th birthday, I look back on the journey from that rooftop during Katrina to today. The path wasn't what I'd expected for my later years. It included more loss, more struggle, more starting over than I could have imagined. But it also brought unexpected gifts, deeper connections, new found purpose, appreciation for life's essentials. Our old neighborhood has partially recovered. Some blocks have new homes built by nonprofits or determined former residents. Other areas remain vacant, reclaimed by nature. Vines and wild flowers covering empty lots where families once lived. The demographic makeup has shifted with fewer of the original residents able to return. Last month, I visited my old school building, finally restored and reoped as a community center. Standing in what used to be my classroom, I felt the weight of both absence and presence. All that was lost, all that has been rebuilt. You look like you've seen a ghost, said a voice behind me. I turned to find Deshaawn. No longer the quiet boy with the drums, but a young man in his 20s working as the cent's music coordinator. Maybe I have, I said, smiling. The ghost of what was? He nodded, understanding. And what could be? That's the story of post Katrina New Orleans and my story, too. Navigating between what was, what is, and what could be. The city will never be the same as it was before the storm. Neither will I. The losses remain real. The neighbors who never returned. The family photographs dissolved in floodwaters. The sense of security washed away. But something new has emerged from the ruins. A hard one wisdom about what truly matters. A deeper connection to place and community. an understanding that home isn't just physical structures, but something we carry within us and rebuild together. When Darlene and I talk about Katrina now, we no longer focus primarily on what was lost. Instead, we speak of what followed, the strangers who became friends, the small kindnesses that sustained us, the communities reformed and strengthened through shared survival. We didn't just survive, Darlene told a reporter doing a 15-year retrospective. We learned how to live differently. That's the unexpected legacy of disaster. Not just destruction, but transformation. Not just what is taken, but what is given. The opportunity to reassess, rebuild, and reimagine our lives with greater awareness of both fragility and strength. I sometimes wonder what my pre- Katrina self would think of my life now. Would he recognize this older man with fewer possessions but a richer understanding of community? Would he understand the path from devastation to this different kind of wholeness? I tell him what I now know. That losing everything doesn't mean the end of everything. That communities can fracture and reform in new patterns. that what seems permanent rarely is. For better and worse, that human connection matters more than physical structures. That music still plays even after the waters rise. These aren't easy lessons, and they came at tremendous cost. But they're the truth I've lived since watching my city and my life transform beneath hurricane winds and floodwaters. They're the story written in New Orleans still unfolding recovery. In the vacant lots and new constructions, in community gardens planted where homes once stood, in second line parades that still wind through changed streets. There, the story written in me. A teacher who lost his classroom but found new ways to share music. A homeowner who lost his house but rediscovered the meaning of home. a New Orleian who watched his city drown but witnessed its determined resurrection. Through it all, I've learned that resilience isn't about bouncing back to what was before. It's about moving forward into something new, carrying what remains after the storms have passed. The core of who we are, the connections that sustain us, the music that plays on the end. For more real life stories, do not forget to like and subscribe.

- How to Protest an Auto Insurance Cancellation, or Non-Renewal Notice: Kind: captions Language: en welcome to the offices of the Maryland insurance administration if you receive a notice of premium increase or a non renewal notice pertaining to your auto policy in Maryland you have the right to file a protest and have an investigator look into your situation the Maryland insurance administration requires insurance providers to send their policyholders a formal notice of a premium increase resulting from a ticket accident or claim after learning about a premium increase due to an ethical accident a consumer submitted his right of protest contesting the premium increase the Maryland insurance administration investigator contacted the insurance carrier who agreed the notice of premium increase did not contain all of the information required by law as a result the premium increase was withdrawn in the original premium amount was reinstated the Maryland insurance administration is the state agency that regulates the insurance industry in the state of Maryland if you ever have any questions or concerns about your insurer feel free to contact us you can find us on Facebook Twitter and LinkedIn or visit our website at [Music] you

  13. @Jiwuwawanco [1 likes]: I disagree regarding your comments on AOB’s being wrong. They can level the playing field in many ways. Storm chasing is a separate issue in my mind anyway.

Both the Insurance business and attorney widely use AOB’s now and have for a long, long time. Get in a wreck in your car, AOB’s are used to pay the body shop. Health insurance uses AOB’s to pay the myriad of professionals that bill you say if you were seriously injured. These are all just contractors in the health field. Attorneys use these when representing clients. Insurance pays them, they disperse the remaining funds. 

Insurance claims went up because AOB’s being used by contractors changes the dynamic. Now rather than having to dupe ignorant homeowners the IC gets to play ball with a knowledgeable professional. That professional knows how to squeeze the lemon to get the best roof possible back on the property at the end of the day.

  1. @GW-gz8jh [1 likes]: To all the people saying they should have gotten flood insurance and this wouldn’t have happened…it actually can. We have flood insurance and several people we know had both wind and flood policies that got caught in a back and forth mess over which was responsible. Often they’re different writers. The company covering wind will say it’s flood damage (even with no flooding in the area) and the company covering flood will say it’s wind. Then both refuse to pay. We had zero flooding in my area but had a limb puncture our flat roof on our home addition causing water damage. Both squabbled back and forth and since it thankfully wasn’t a life changing amount we ended up paying to have it fixed ourselves than litigated after. Just because you have the policies doesn’t mean you will see any money or that it won’t take legal wrangling to get it.

- Why do hurricanes have names?: Kind: captions Language: en why do we name hurricanes I'm stormshield meteorologist Jason Meyers let's find out the short answer is this it's a whole heck of a lot easier and less confusing when we name hurricanes places that see a lot of hurricane activity like the West Indies have been naming them since the middle of the 19th century they'd Nam these storms after saints whose day occurred nearest to win the hurricane made landfall at the end of the 19th century an Australian meteorologist was naming storms too but instead of saints he mostly used the names of politicians because he couldn't think of anyone else who wandered around aimlessly and blew hot air the rest of us were identifying these storms by their latitude and longitude which as you can imagine wasn't very helpful for anyone not aware of their exact location at all times not to mention it's a lot harder to say 21° North 70° West as compared to Hurricane Earl it'd get even more confusing if there were two storms imagine throwing out all those coordinates and expecting people to follow occasionally these storms would be named after the fact usually named after something or some place it destroyed not very helpful when you're trying to keep people safe ahead of time eventually meteorologists started unofficially naming these huge destructive storms after their wives whether whether that was meant to be a compliment or an insult I have no idea in 1950 we got serious and started naming storms in alphabetical order it was based on military terms think Abel Baker Charlie dog and so on that only lasted 3 years it became a little confusing so in 1953 we started using female names in alphabetical order similar to what we use today by 1979 we started alternating women and men's names we didn't want to be sexist now six different lists are used in rotation some of those storm names will be retired if there is especially deadly or expensive think Katrina or Andrew if that's the case the World Meteorological organization gets together and picks replacement names chosen from the different countries that could be affected by these natural disasters for stormshield I'm meteorologist Jason Meyers

- THE SUPERCELL - MOTHER OF STORMS: Kind: captions Language: en There are different breeds of thunderstorms each with their own characteristics There are single-celled storms often weak and short-lived Multi-celled storms sporadic and unorganized and and squalls organizing into dramatic lines they can stretch hundreds of miles But the mother of all thunder storms is the supercell Supercells are the least common mode of storms and Their defining character is a deep persistently rotating updraft called a mesocyclone Super cells are often referred to as rotating thunderstorms This rotating nature enables them to generate torrential rainfall and severe winds Intense and continuous lightning activity Enormous hail And it's super cells that are responsible for producing the majority of the world's violent tornadoes Supercells can occur all over the world but in one location they turn up with alarming frequency Tornado alley in the United States Fewer than 30 percent of super cells produce tornadoes And scientists are still trying to understand why one supercell produces violet tornadoes And another does not Supercells come in three different classifications. The low precipitation supercell or LP The classic supercell And the high precipitation supercell or HP where heavy rainfall shroud much of the internal storm structure They often form as one mode and transition to another Once a supercell is on the roll it can dominate the atmosphere persisting for hours and hours Sometimes the atmosphere generates mixed modes Storms with characteristics of other storm types Squalls with super cells embedded in their lines or hybrid cells with both the squall and supercell structures No two super cells are exactly the same With their explosive energy and wind shear sculpted beauty Being in their presence can be captivating or terrifying. Or both. They are a dream scenario for nature photographers And time-lapse photography truly captures their beauty the mesocyclone twists the supercell into dramatic sky scapes. A storm that resembles stacked plates With tiers like upside down wedding cake Or the alien invading mothership With the most powerful supercells time-lapse photography isn't necessary to capture their intense churning The clouds move so fast the storm doesn't appear from this world

moon well it all comes down to gravity the moon has about 1% of the mass of the Earth which means that it doesn't have enough gravity to hold on to any gas atmosphere anything that it did have would have been blown away by the solar wind billions of years ago in fact we did a whole episode on what it would take to terraform the moon and it turns out you need to constantly replenish the atmosphere in fact this is one of the reasons why the Martian atmosphere is so thin it was probably thicker in the past but the solar wind stripped off all the lighter atmosphere long ago now it's just 1% the thickness of the Earth's atmosphere now I've said that the moon has almost no atmosphere but that almost no means partly yes there is in fact an incredibly thin atmosphere surrounding the moon which measures about a 100 trillionth the thickness of the Earth's atmosphere now there are a few sources of this atmosphere first there's volcanic outgassing that comes from the Moon and this this contributes a little helium and radon and then there's the constant microm meteorite bombardment that kicks up pulverized lunar regolith but perhaps the strangest atmospheric feature is a storm that does rage across the surface of the Moon right at the Terminator the exact line between the Moon's day side and its night side it turns out the day Side of the Moon is positively charged and the night side is negative ly charged as the Terminator moves the polarity of the Dust flips and drives it sideways in fact the astronauts who walked on the moon actually reported seeing this they saw bands or
