- 10 Florida Cities Where the Insurance Crisis Is So Bad Owners Are Walking Away From Their Homes: Kind: captions Language: en Here is what the Florida real estate story is not telling you. The statewide numbers look fine. Prices up, people moving in, the Sunbelt narrative holding together on paper. But city by city, something different is happening. Homeowners are walking away. Not because the market collapsed, because their insurance carrier sent a non-renewal letter, because the replacement policy costs three times as much, because a house that penciled out in 2021 no longer pencils out in 2026 once you add what insurance actually costs now. This is not a prediction. This is not a future risk. This is happening right now across Florida. Today, we are counting down 10 cities where the insurance crisis is so severe that owners are abandoning their homes. If you own Florida property, you need to watch this. If you are thinking about buying, you really need to watch this. Let us get into it. Cape Coral is the largest city by land area in Florida outside Jacksonville. Over 200,000 people, miles of canals, waterfront lots, the kind of suburban Florida dream that brought retirees and remote workers flooding in during the pandemic years. Prices jumped hard. The median home that sold for 280,000 in 2019 was pushing 450,000 by 2022. Investors bought. Families stretching their budgets bought. And then the insurance industry looked at Cape Coral's flood maps, its hurricane exposure, its canal front density, and started doing its own math. The average homeowner's insurance premium in Cape Coral has gone from roughly $4,000 a year in 2019 to over $11,000 in 2026, according to the Insurance Information Institute. That is a 275% increase in 7 years. On a $400,000 mortgage at 6 and 1/2%, the monthly principal and interest is already around $2,500. Add $11,000 a year in insurance and you are adding another $900 per month. Flood insurance, which is separate and required for most Cape Coral properties, adds another $2,000 to $4,000 annually. The total monthly housing cost for a median Cape Coral home is now pushing $4,500 on a city where the median household income is around $68,000 a year. That math does not work. Over 19% of homes listed for sale in Cape Coral have had their prices cut, according to Redfin. Sellers are not cutting because they want to. They are cutting because buyers are running the full numbers now. Cape Coral is not emptying out, but the insurance crisis has permanently repriced what the canal lifestyle actually costs. Port St. Lucie sits on Florida's Treasure Coast, about an hour north of West Palm Beach. Population over 230,000. The pitch was always the same. Florida weather, space, a house that costs a fraction of what it costs in Miami, and close enough to the coast to feel like a good decision. That pitch worked for a long time. Then the insurance carriers looked at Port St. Lucie's wind exposure and its claims history after a string of active hurricane seasons, and they started leaving. Three major carriers have stopped writing new homeowner policies in Port St. Lucie's zip codes since 2023, according to the Florida Office of Insurance Regulation. When carriers exit a market, the homeowners left behind face a version of musical chairs where the remaining players charge more because they are absorbing more risk. Premiums in Port St. Lucie have risen an average of 41% in 2 years. The overvaluation rate sits at 14.2% according to Zillow, meaning homes are priced about 14% above what local income fundamentals can sustain. When you layer an insurance spike on top of an already overvalued market, you get exactly what Port St. Lucie is showing right now. 21% of all active listings have had price reductions. Days on market are stretching past 60 days on properties that would have gone under contract in a week 3 years ago. The sellers are still hoping the 2022 buyer shows up. That buyer is not coming back at these prices with these insurance bills attached. Lehigh Acres sits just east of Fort Myers, deep in Lee County, one of the hardest hit areas in Florida after Hurricane Ian made landfall in September 2022. The storm caused over $112 billion in damage, the costliest hurricane in Florida history. And in Lehigh Acres, the aftermath revealed something that had been quietly building for years. The insurance market was already breaking before Ian hit. Ian just accelerated everything. Seven Florida-based insurance companies went insolvent between 2022 and 2024, according to the Florida Department of Financial Services. Seven carriers gone. Policyholders were automatically transferred to Citizens Property Insurance, the state-backed insurer of last resort. As of early 2026, Citizens carries over 1.2 million policies, according to its own published data. That is not a safety net anymore. That is the primary insurer for a significant portion of the Florida market. And Citizens premiums are rising under a state-mandated glide path specifically designed to push policyholders back to a private market that does not want them at any price homeowners can afford. In Lehigh Acres, the median home price sits at around $260,000. That sounds affordable until you add an insurance bill averaging $9,000 to $13,000 annually. For a buyer putting 10% down, the monthly insurance cost alone can exceed $1,000. That is before the mortgage. That is before property taxes. 23% of active listings have had price reductions. Some have been reduced twice. The buyers who would have stretched for this market in 2021 are running the full numbers now, and the full numbers do not work. Seven insurers gone, 1.2 million policies pushed onto a state backstop that was never designed to hold them, and a median home that costs more to insure each year than many Americans spend on a car. Lehigh Acres is not collapsing, >> [music] >> but the insurance math here stopped working long before most owners realized it. The assumption most people make about Florida's insurance crisis is that it is a coastal problem. Stay inland, stay away from the beach, and you are safe [music] from the worst of it. Kissimmee is the city that breaks that assumption completely. Kissimmee sits in Osceola County, about 30 minutes south of Orlando. Population over 80,000 in the city proper. It is not on the coast. It is not in a named flood zone for most of its residential neighborhoods. By every intuitive measure, Kissimmee should be insulated from what is happening elsewhere in Florida. It is not. Homeowners insurance premiums in Kissimmee have risen an average of 63% since 2021, according to the Florida Office of Insurance Regulation. Not because of flood maps, because the statewide reinsurance market that backs every Florida insurer, coastal or inland, has repriced Florida risk as a category. When reinsurers, the companies that insure the insurance companies, decided that Florida as a whole represents outsized hurricane risk, every carrier in every Florida zip code felt it. Kissimmee homeowners are paying for the coast even though they do not live on it. The overvaluation problem compounds the insurance problem here. Kissimmee's median home price sits at around $310,000, according to Zillow, against a median household income of approximately $54,000. That is a price-to-income ratio of nearly 5.8. The general rule of thumb for a healthy housing market is three to four. Kissimmee is running nearly double that. 22% of all active listings have had price reductions, according to Redfin. The investors who bought for short-term rental income near the Orlando tourism corridor are now selling into a market where the next buyer is running the same broken numbers. Kissimmee is not collapsing. But the premise that inland Florida is immune to the insurance crisis just took a serious hit. The reinsurance market does not care where your street is. It has repriced the entire state. Ocala is a city of about 67,000 people in Marion County, roughly in the geographic center of Florida. Horse country. Known for its farms, its springs, [music] its relative affordability. During the pandemic, Ocala attracted buyers priced out of Tampa, Orlando, and Jacksonville. Home prices rose sharply. The median jumped from around 160,000 in 2019 to over 260,000 by 2022. A 62% increase in 3 years. And unlike coastal markets, Ocala's insurance picture looked manageable during that run-up. It seemed like the kind of Florida market that offered the lifestyle without the exposure. Then, the condominium crisis arrived. The Champlain Towers South collapse in Surfside in 2021 changed Florida law. New structural inspection requirements for condominiums over three stories and older than 30 years became mandatory. The inspections revealed deferred maintenance problems across hundreds of Florida condo buildings that associations had been quietly ignoring. Insurers, looking at the inspection reports, started either refusing to write policies on affected buildings or pricing those policies at levels that made monthly condo ownership in Ocala look nothing like the purchase price suggested. Condo owners in Ocala are receiving special assessment notices for structural repairs running from 20,000 to over $60,000 per unit, according to Florida Realtors. >> For retirees on fixed incomes who bought Ocala condos specifically because they were affordable, a $50,000 special assessment is not a line item. It is a crisis. The inventory of Ocala condos has risen 31% year-over-year. Days on market have stretched past 75 days on units that were selling in 2 weeks in 2022. Buyers are reading the HOA disclosure documents, seeing the pending assessments and the insurance costs baked into the monthly fees, and pulling out of contracts. Ocala is not crashing. But, the affordable inland retirement story that drove this market for years has been repriced by inspection requirements and insurance costs that no one saw coming when they signed. If you are still here watching insurance bills dismantle Florida's housing market city by city, we are the same kind of people. Subscribe. This channel covers Florida every single day, and the numbers only get more interesting from here. Daytona Beach sits on Florida's northeast coast, Volusia County, population around 72,000. One of the most recognizable names in Florida, synonymous with affordable coastal access that made it attractive to buyers who could not afford prices further south. The median home sits at around $240,000, cheap by Florida coastal standards. That affordability made Daytona Beach a consistent target for investors and buyers looking for value on the water. The problem is that coastal affordability in Florida in 2026 means something specific to insurance carriers. It means wind mitigation requirements, flood zone designations, and carriers still writing policies. Charging rates that reflect what a bad hurricane season actually costs. Annual homeowners insurance premiums in Daytona Beach coastal zip codes are now averaging between 14,000 and $19,000, depending on the age of the home, Insurance Regulation. For a buyer purchasing a $240,000 home with 20% down, the mortgage at 6 and 1/2% runs about $1,200 a month. The insurance alone adds between 1,100 and $1,500 per month on top of that. The total monthly payment before taxes and utilities is now between 2,300 and $2,700. On Volusia County's median household income of around $58,000, that [music] payment consumes over 50% of gross monthly income. Lenders cap debt-to-income ratios at 43% for most conventional loans. The insurance cost alone is pushing buyers out of qualification before they factor in property taxes. 26% of all active listings in Daytona Beach have had price reductions, according to Redfin. More than one in four sellers conceding that their original asking price assumed a buyer who could absorb these carrying costs. The cash flow that justified investor purchases here no longer exists once you run insurance plus management fees plus the softening of post-pandemic travel demand. They are selling. The buyers they hope to find are looking at the full monthly cost and choosing differently. The affordable coastal access story that drove this market for years has been fundamentally repriced. And in Daytona Beach, that repricing is showing up in every listing that just cut its price for the second time this quarter. The shock entry on this list is not a small coastal town or an overheated market. It is Jacksonville. The largest city in Florida by land area, population over 1 million, the economic anchor of northeast Florida with a deepwater port, >> four military installations, a financial services sector, health care, and logistics. Jacksonville is supposed to be the kind of city that weathers corrections because the economic base is too diversified for one factor to dominate. That assumption is being tested right now. Jacksonville's insurance market has deteriorated faster than most residents expected. The same reinsurance repricing that hit Kissimmee hit Jacksonville. The same carrier exits that stripped options from Port St. Lucie stripped options from Jacksonville neighborhoods east of I-95, where older housing stock sits closer to the St. Johns River flood plain. In Duval County, the number of active insurance carriers writing new homeowner policies dropped from 22 in 2020 to 11 in 2026, according to the Florida Office of Insurance Regulation. When you cut the competitive field in half, premiums do not stay flat. Average annual premiums in Jacksonville have increased 68% since 2021. On a city where the median home price is approximately $312,000 and the median household income is around 62,000, that increase is not absorbed quietly. It shows up in the monthly payment math that buyers run before they make offers. The military presence that Jacksonville residents cite as a stabilizing force has its own insurance dimension rarely discussed. Service members using VA loans are discovering that VA loan approval does not insulate them from the insurance market. The loan is guaranteed. The insurance premium is not. A service member qualifying for a $300,000 VA purchase in Jacksonville in 2026 is looking at an insurance bill that has no relationship to what that same property cost to insure in 2020. Over 20% of active Jacksonville listings have had price reductions, according to Zillow. Days on market have stretched to an average of 61 days, up from 23 days in 2022. Jacksonville has not lost its economic momentum, but it has lost the buyer confidence that defined it for half a decade, and the insurance bill is the primary reason why. Sarasota has spent years at the top of every best places to live in Florida list. The art scene, the Gulf beaches, the relative sophistication compared to other coastal markets. It attracted affluent retirees, remote workers with equity from California and the northeast, second home buyers who wanted Gulf access without the chaos of Naples. For years, that premium demand kept Sarasota insulated from corrections hitting cheaper markets. The logic was that premium buyers could absorb higher costs. Premium buyers have limits, too. Sarasota County sits on the Gulf of Mexico. Hurricane Ian made landfall roughly 60 miles south in 2022. The storm track spared Sarasota from the worst of the surge, but the insurance market did not spare Sarasota from the aftermath. Carriers that had been underwriting Gulf Coast exposure for decades looked at Ian's damage totals, looked at their reinsurance costs for the following year, and began repricing or exiting. Annual homeowners insurance premiums for a median Sarasota home, valued at approximately $550,000, according to Redfin, now range from 18,000 to $31,000 annually, depending on the home's age, construction type, and distance from the water. On a $550,000 and 1/2% runs approximately $2,700 a month. Insurance at the median of roughly $20,000 annually adds another $1,667 per month. The total monthly payment before property taxes is now pushing $4,400. Sarasota County property taxes on a median home add another $600 to $750 monthly. The full carrying cost of a median Sarasota home in 2026 is approaching $65,000 a year. That is more than the median household income of most American cities. Entirely spent on one house. The ultra-wealthy end of the Sarasota market, homes above 2 million, is still moving. Buyers at that level are not financing and are carrying insurance as a rounding error, but the broad middle of the market, $500 to $900,000, where retiring professionals and equity-rich relocators were competing 2 years ago, is showing real strain. 31% of active Sarasota listings have had price reductions, according to Redfin, nearly one in three sellers. The insurance market does not grade on a curve for desirability. It prices for risk. And Sarasota's Gulf Coast risk profile is expensive, regardless of how beautiful the sunsets are. If you are still watching at number three, as Florida's most desirable market start showing up on this list, subscribe. The top two are the ones that will change how you think about this state entirely. Tampa is the number two entry, and it is the one that shocks people. Major League sports, a booming downtown, financial services, healthcare, the University of South Florida. Tampa's economic story over the past decade has been one of the most compelling in the Sunbelt. The median home price hit approximately $400,000 at the peak in 2022. In 2026, it feels like a number the market cannot sustain. Tampa sits on a bay that the National Hurricane Center has identified as one of the most vulnerable major metropolitan areas in the country to a direct hurricane strike because of the bay's geometry and the density of low-lying development surrounding it. Insurance carriers are not ignoring that research. They are pricing it. Average annual homeowner's insurance premiums in Tampa have reached $14,000 to $22,000, depending on location and home age, For a buyer purchasing a $400,000 home with 20% down, the mortgage runs approximately $2,200 a month. Insurance at the median adds $1,500 per month. The total monthly payment before property taxes is $3,700. On Hillsborough County's median $66,000, that payment consumes 67% of gross monthly income. That is not a mortgage. That is the entire paycheck. Realtor.com projected a negative 2.1% price decline for the Tampa metro in 2026, the first projected annual decline for a market that spent years as one of the fastest appreciating in America. On a $400,000 home, that is $8,400 in lost value in a single year. For homeowners who bought at the peak with minimal down payments, that decline is not just a paper loss. It is equity that no longer exists as a cushion between what they owe and what the home is worth. 24% of active Tampa listings have had The condo market is struggling under the combined weight of structural inspection requirements, rising HOA fees, insurance costs that have made older Tampa buildings nearly uninsurable at rational prices, and a buyer pool that has stepped back entirely. Tampa is not going to stop being Tampa, but the math of owning property here in 2026 is a fundamentally different calculation than it was in 2021. And for anyone who bought at the peak expecting Tampa prices to only go up, that negative projection is not just a number on a screen. It is real money that is no longer in their pocket. Number one on this list is the entry that will surprise people, not because Miami is unknown, because Miami was supposed to be the exception, the international city, the luxury destination, the market that foreign buyers, hedge funds, and wealthy relocators from New York and California turned into one of the hottest real estate stories in the country between 2020 and 2023. Miami was supposed to transcend the problems hitting everywhere else because demand was global, supply was constrained, and the buyer pool had no ceiling. That story is changing right now. Miami-Dade recorded a negative 4.3% median home price decline in 2025, according to Redfin data. In a county where the median home price sits at approximately $620,000, negative 4.3% is $26,600 in lost value in 1 year, gone on paper. That is a down payment on a home in most American cities, evaporated from the balance sheet of owners who bought at or near the peak. And the force driving that decline is the same force that has appeared in every city on this list, scaled to match Miami's price points. Homeowner's insurance premiums in Miami-Dade now average between $24,000 and $42,000 annually for properties at the county median, according to the The range is that wide because it depends on the age of the building, the floor, if it is a condo, the distance from the coast, and which carrier is still willing to write the policy at all. For many Miami properties, particularly condos built before 2000, the answer to that last question is Citizens, the state-backed insurer of last resort, because the private market has effectively exited those buildings entirely. A 40-year-old Miami condo carrying a $600,000 price tag and a $42,000 annual insurance bill is not a $600,000 asset. It is a liability structured to look like an asset until the renewal notice arrives. The Surfside collapse and the subsequent Florida condo inspection laws have layered a specific additional cost onto Miami's condo market that does not exist in the same way for single-family homes. Remediation costs in Miami are running from $50,000 to over $120,000 per unit in the most severely affected buildings, according to Florida Realtors Association reporting. Owners who cannot afford the special assessment are selling. Owners who can afford it are questioning whether a building that just levied a $100,000 assessment is a sound long-term investment. The condo inventory in Miami-Dade has risen 43% year over year, according to Zillow. Days on market for condos now average 89 days, up from 26 days in 2022. That is three times as long to find a buyer. Three times as long means three more months of insurance premiums, three more months of HOA fees on a building that may levy another assessment before the current one is paid off. The international buyer who was supposed to insulate Miami from domestic corrections still exists at the luxury tier. Cash buyers from Latin America, Europe, and the Middle East are still [music] closing deals in new construction with modern wind mitigation and known manageable insurance figures. But the resale market, the broad middle of Miami-Dade, where working professionals and first-time buyers were supposed to participate, has lost [music] that buyer pool almost entirely. A household earning Miami-Dade's median income of approximately $68,000 cannot qualify for a mortgage on a $600,000 home and cannot absorb a $30,000 annual insurance bill. The math is not close. It is not a matter of stretching. It simply does not work. 38% of active Miami-Dade listings have had price reductions, according to Redfin. More than one in three sellers acknowledging that the price they started with assumed a buyer who no longer exists at that number. Some of those sellers will eventually find their buyer. Many will not. Miami is not going to become affordable. It is not going to stop being Miami, but the idea that Miami real estate is a one-way ticket took a direct hit from an insurance market that does not care about the view from the 42nd floor. A negative 4.3% decline in the most celebrated market in Florida is not a footnote. It is a signal. The insurance crisis is not a coastal problem, or an inland problem, or a condo problem, or a single-family problem. It is a Florida problem, and it has reached the top of the market. Those are 10 Florida cities that owners are walking away from their homes. From Cape Coral to Kissimmee to Miami, the statewide headlines about population growth and rising prices are real. But behind those numbers, there are real cities, real insurance bills, and real people making the calculation that the home they bought is no longer the financial decision they thought they were making.

This is the invisible system. [music] Insurance is not designed to cover everything. It is designed to cover specific [music] risks and avoid others. That's how the business works. The more predictable [music] the risk, the easier it is to insure. The more uncertain, the easier it is to exclude. And that's not an exception. It's the rule. The days of a roofer suing your insurance company for $50,000 over a $5,000 leak are over. Second, we had a mild hurricane season in 2024. Carriers actually made a profit. Third, reinsurance costs dropped. The insurance for the insurance companies got cheaper. So, the gap between cheap citizens and the expensive private market is closing. You finally have choices. But choices do come with complexity. Here's the first place you're going to get tripped up. You see that Patriot Select is $300 cheaper than your current policy. You think, "I'll cancel today and switch." In insurance, there are two ways to get your money back when you cancel midyear. Pratta and short rate. If the insurance company cancels you, they have to give you a prata refund. That means if you used 50% of the policy time, you get exactly 50% of your money back. That's pretty fair. But in Florida, if you cancel the policy midterm, meaning before your renewal date, the insurance company is legally allowed to use a short rate cancellation table. That's a penalty. They keep a percentage of your unearned premium to cover their administrative costs. Here's the math that ruins your day. Let's say you have six months left on a $4,000 policy. That is $2,000 of unearned money you expect back, but they hit you with a short rate. They might keep 10% of that remaining balance. That's $200 gone. If your new policy only saves you $150, congratulations. You just paid $50 for the privilege of filling out some paperwork. The strategy is simple. Shop now. Lock in the new rate, but set the effective date for the exact day your old policy expires. This is called nonrenewing. There's no penalty for non-renewing. Only switch midterm if the savings are substantial. I'm talking 15% or more. Now, losing $200 on a cancellation fee is pretty annoying, but getting kicked off your insurance against your will, that's happening right now to thousands of people, and many don't even realize it until their mortgage payment goes up. Citizens was never meant to be permanent. It's the state's backup plan, the insurer of last resort. So when private carriers come back, the state actively pushes you towards them. And here's the thing, they can legally block you from renewing your citizens policy, even if the private option is more expensive. I want to know if this has happened to you. The state wants citizens property insurance to shrink. It's too big. So, they created the 20% rule. Here's how it works for most homeowners. If a private insurance carrier like Slide or Florida Peninsula offers to ensure your house and their price is within 20% of the citizens renewal price, you are ineligible for citizens. Let's look at some math. Say your citizens renewal is $3,000. A private company offers you a policy for $3,500. Now 3,500 is exactly 16.7% higher than 3,000. that is under the 20% threshold. The result, you must take the $3500 policy. You cannot stay with citizens. You just got a forced $500 rate hike. Now, there are some exceptions and an appeal process, but for most homeowners, this is how it works. And here's the kicker. If you don't respond to that letter, if you throw it away thinking it's spam, you're automatically switched to the private carrier. You wake up one day and your mortgage company is paying a higher rate to a company you've never heard of. This brings me to the teaser rate risk. Some of these new carriers are offering rates that are strictly designed to be just under that 20% threshold to pull you away from citizens. Most Florida carriers are rated by Demote Tech. It's the industry standard and honestly, it's relatively easy to get a good rating from them. Weiss safety ratings are much tougher. They're the ones that actually predict financial trouble before it happens. If a company has a great demo tech rating, but a terrible Weiss rating, that's a red flag. Second, look at claim denial rates. Some carriers approve 95% of claims. Others deny 40%. If a storm hits, you want to know who actually pays out. That data is public. We just don't talk about it enough. Third, look at years in the Florida market. Okay, enough about the traps. Let's talk about how you can take control. If you're tired of waiting for the governor to lower your rates, here are three things you can do to force your premiums down yourself. Optimization number one, the 5-year expiration date. Most people don't know this, but your wind mitigation inspection expires. In Florida, this inspection is the golden ticket. It proves your roof is nailed down properly and your trusses are strapped. It can save you 40% or more on the wind portion of your premium. The catch is that it's only valid for 5 years. If you had an inspection done in 2020 and it expired in 2025, when your policy renews this year, the insurance company's computer will automatically remove those discounts. Your rate could jump $1,000 overnight. Not because rates went up, but because your paperwork expired. The fix is simple. Check the date on your windmill form. If it is 5 years old, pay the $150 to get a new one. It pays for itself instantly. Optimization number two, the plumbing investment. If your home was built between the late '7s and the mid90s, go look under your sink. If you see gray pipes, you likely have polybutylene. Insurers hate these pipes. They burst from the inside out. Most preferred carriers, the ones that offering the low rates, they will deny you outright or hit you with a massive search charge if you have poly pipes. You're forced onto expensive substandard policies. The insider tip here is that you're going to have to replace these pipes when you sell the house anyway. No buyer in 2026 will touch a house with poly pipes without a massive price reduction. A whole home repipe to PEX cost about $6,000 to $15,000 depending on the size of your home. If you do it now, you unlock the cheap insurance rates immediately. You save money every month and you increase the resale value of your home. Don't wait until listing day. Get the savings now. Optimization number three, the roof age sweet spot. Finally, the roof. In Florida, a shingle roof over 15 years old is a liability. You're paying a search charge just for having it. If your roof is approaching that age, do the math. A new roof might cost $15,000, but it could drop your insurance premium by $2,000 a year. Over just 7 years, that roof pays for itself, and you still have eight plus years of savings after that. Plus, newer roofs qualify for the best mitigation credits. If you have the cash, a new roof is one of the best financial investments you can make in this insurance market. There's a lot of moving parts here. New carriers, expiring inspections, depopulation letters. You need to know which of these 83 companies filing for rate cuts are actually safe and which ones are just cheap. I've compiled a 2026 Florida insurance report card. It lists the major carriers and their Weiss safety rating, which is much tougher than the standard demo tech rating everyone uses. I also included the claim denial rates for 2024, so you can see who actually pays out when a storm. - Big Changes for Florida Insurance? Orlando Homeowners MUST See This!: Kind: captions Language: en The Orlando area is once again facing a shortage Skyrocketing premiums, companies going belly up, and people across the area caught in the middle [music] as the state's homeowners insurance market enters what some are calling a free fall. So, what is the state doing about this? A little more than two years ago, every single news outlet in the country was reporting how Florida's insurance market was literally collapsing. Insurance companies were going insolvent left and right. Citizens Insurance, the insurer of last resort for Florida, was bloated with more than 1.4 million policies, way more than they're supposed to manage. It was not unheard of for homeowners to get a 60% rate increase in a single year. Stories abound how people were literally being forced out of their homes. Well, folks, I'm back with an update. And I've got to tell you something that's going to literally sound absolutely insane [music] after everything the Sunshine State has been through as it relates to insurance. For the first time in over a decade, insurance rates in Florida are going down. The population of folks relying on Citizens is shrinking. New insurance companies are now flooding back to the state. And if you live in Orlando, you might actually catch a break this year. But before you pop the champagne bottle, we need to talk about what's really happening because there's a massive threat brewing in Tallahassee. There are things in the works that could literally reverse everything we know about insurance. Because today we're going to talk about exactly what it's like to get insurance today throughout the state and specifically in Central Florida. So, that if you live here now, you can analyze whether or not you're fully receiving all the benefits. And there is a lot of new and interesting laws coming down the pipe this year that you absolutely need to know about because they're going to be game-changers for your life. And for some, not in a great way. So, if you're here for it, let's get right into the update. For those of you that are new to the conversation or you weren't in Florida, let me give you a quick recap of exactly how broken Florida insurance really was. When this crisis reached its peak, Florida alone had more than 80% of all homeowner insurance lawsuits in the entire country happening in just one state. Now, we only had 9% of the claims, but 80% of the lawsuits. Now, the math doesn't work unless something's deeply wrong. So, what was it? Four things. Attorneys gaming so-called one-way fee structures, roofers running AOB scams to defraud insurance companies, general contractors doing the exact same thing, and frankly a whole lot of homeowners who got talked into filing bogus claims. Billions of dollars were getting siphoned out of the insurance system every single year, and it all got passed back onto us in the form of skyrocketing premiums. What happened next was a perfect storm. A literal series of hurricanes combined with massive COVID inflation, and all of this cost bogging down the system, and you had insurance going insolvent left and right. Many others completely pulling out of the state and not even competing for business here. The state-backed Citizens Insurance ballooned to 1.42 million policies because no one else would write coverage. If you could even get insurance, you were paying $6,000 to $8,000, sometimes more than $10,000 a year. And at this time, if you tried selling your house, good luck because buyers couldn't get mortgage without insurance, and insurance companies were rejecting applications or demanding full roof replacements. Roofs that had 12, 14, 15 years of life left, they're saying it's time to put a new roof on if you want me to cover it. It was a nightmare, a complete and total disaster. But, let's jump to where we are today. Well, Citizens Property Insurance Corporation just approved in a statewide rate decrease of 8.7% for 2026. Over 330,000 policy holders across all 67 counties are seeing their rates drop. Get this, more than 150,000 of them are actually getting reductions of 10% or more. And in South Florida, Miami-Dade, Broward, Palm Beach, where litigation was the absolute worst, homeowners are seeing average decreases from 11 to 14% and just for citizens alone this marks the first meaningful rate decrease going back to 2015. Get this for over a decade the trajectory of Florida insurance by and large year over year has been up. It's only one way every year it cost you more and now the trend is being reversed and that's not even the craziest part. The craziest part is what happened to bloated insurance population. There were way too many people enrolled on the state backed policy way too many for it to be safe for the state. There are almost 1.5 million policy holders as we approach the year 2023. Now as of January 2026 the number of policy holders is down almost 400,000 policies in just over 2 years. Now why did that happen? Where did they go? There was actually a massive move in the private insurance market. Remember at the peak of the collapse we had around 40 insurers in the entire state. a healthy number for the entire market for you to go and shop and get competitive numbers. Well, that number with collapsing insurance companies and companies leaving the market it was cut around half and many of the ones that remained themselves were on the brink of collapse. So my friends for the first time in years there's more business here wanting to do business in Florida again because of the protections and reforms that were passed. 17 new companies have entered the market. Companies like Orion 180, Mainsail, Tailrow, Mangrove, Trident. These are carriers who looked at Florida saw the reforms working and said okay, we'll come back. We'll take the risks on at this point. My friends are not just entering the market. They're competing. Florida Peninsula filed for an 8.4% rate decrease. Patriot Select filed for an 11.3% rate decrease. Heritage Property dropped rates by 10% and many of their inland counties. Universal Property and Casualty cut rates by around 5%. State Farms reported cumulative reductions of nearly 20% across multiple filings for the counties they cover. The data doesn't lie, but here's what you need to understand. This didn't just happen because insurance companies suddenly became more generous. This happened because two very specific things aligned at the exact same time. Number one, the tort reforms that passed in 2022 and 2023 are finally working. When the legislature eliminated one-way attorney fees and killed the AOB assignment of benefit scam, the lawsuit industry collapsed. Insurance litigation in Florida has dropped by over 70% since those reforms took effect. And you'll remember how I told you that attorneys were running billboards all over the state promising free roof replacements through your insurance. That has all but stopped. And many of you that live here now, you'll remember that after every thunderstorm or hail storm came through, you had a roofer knocking on your door saying, "You might qualify for a new roof." That has dropped off immensely. The floodgates of fraudulent claims and frivolous lawsuits closed, and suddenly insurance companies could actually predict their losses again. And when you can predict losses, you can price policies accurately. Orlando area homeowners, don't pay too much when you list your house. One of the other new factors to consider is roofs. That means, "Jared, I I have a nineteen year old roof on this house I'm going to buy." Now, let's compare Central Florida to what I'm hearing is happening in Palm Beach County or Miami or the coastal areas. The same comparable insurance cost you get here might run you in from 7 to $9,000 on the upper end in those other areas. Higher risk coastal properties are still paying double or triple what inland properties are actually having to pay, and the gap has actually widened over the past year. The insurance companies have gotten very sophisticated about risk modeling, and they know exactly how much more dangerous it is to insure a home that's 5 miles from the coast versus one that's 50 miles from the coast. So, if you're in Orlando, you're 50 miles from the coast, you're in the sweet spot. You have lower wind risk, and you have a lower risk of flooding. Now, I just had the chance to interview one of the most knowledgeable insurance brokers I know who has a thorough understanding of all the current residential carriers. Third, and this is crucial, if you have a Citizens policy, understand that you are now required by Florida law to carry flood insurance, even if you're not in a flood zone. This is a law that passed back in 2022 that's now taking effect in 2026. The new law states that if you have a replacement cost of over $400,000 on your house, you must have flood coverage if you're with Citizens insurance. So, pay attention to that. It's kicking in this year. All right, team, the next thing we need to talk about is the new laws coming up in 2026 that are going to affect your insurance, plus some special hacks and grants that you've got to know about. They're out there right now that you can take advantage of, fully funded with millions of dollars to help you save on your insurance. First up, there is a new requirement for full replacement value insurance. And what does that mean? It means that your insurance company has to provide a policy that will actually cover the cost to completely rebuild your home, not some depreciated value. This, of course, will protect homeowners from being under insured, which has been a massive problem after people's home values surged and they didn't adjust their policies. Then they finally find out they get hit by a hurricane and they're surprised to find out they don't have enough money coming from the pockets of the insurance company to fix their home. It's a travesty. The insurance companies would say, "Hey, you just had to call us and change it." And most people don't know to do this. But my friends, the trade-off with flood replacement is that it costs more, and that's why people weren't calling in to actually change it in the first place. You get better protection, but you're also covering a higher amount to replace your house, which will in turn cost you more money. The new laws also changed the cancellation rules for roofing contracts. The reason why is because insurance companies were notorious for canceling policies or refusing to renew people because of roof age. So, even if the roof was in perfectly good condition, people were getting stonewalled. Now there's stricter requirements in state law as to how and when they can actually do that. I was telling you earlier, if you meet certain income requirements, there is help of up to $10,000 in grant dollars to help improve your house to lower your insurance cost. And a huge thing to watch this cycle in 2026, some legislators want to bring back one-way attorney fees. One of the laws coming through 2026 is asking for a cap on insurance prices of 10 to 15% max. Now you've probably heard me talk about this on the channel before. This has been proposed a couple of times. The rate cap proposal has concerns, probably has limited chance of passing here in Florida. So, there's a third line item for deductibles that uh insurance companies have come out with. And what I see a lot of times is if someone's shopping insurance, especially for a new purchase or they're a first-time homeowner, they're just looking at the bottom line as far as what the price is um that's being quoted. But not all insurance companies are created equal and they're not all quoting the same deductibles with only having two deductibles because some have come out with the third deductible now. So, what you need to look for is number one, the all other peril deductible. That typically is going to be $1,000 or $2,500. Um, that is for any type of peril that could cause damage to your property. So, for example, a lightning strike or a water damage or fire. Those are perils that can cause damage. That's just a flat per occurrence deductible. So, anytime you have a claim, you'd be responsible to pay that deductible. insurance company would pay everything above and beyond that. Um the next one that everybody is pretty familiar with is hurricane deductibles. Um the stand higher than your general deductible. >> Typically going to be higher unless you have newer construction. You can often times get them at a,000 or 2500, >> okay, >> on the hurricane deductible. But most companies in the industry are going to give a 2% hurricane deductible, which they calculate that off of your dwelling coverage. So if you're insured for 500,000 on the dwelling, they're going to say you're responsible for 2% of that before we pay anything. And that's for a category one hurricane or above. If it's a tropical storm, a windstorm, or anything lower, that falls under your all other peril deductible category. And the third one that's pretty new to the industry, um, some companies are coming out with, uh, wind/hale deductibles for a third line item. >> Um, so you got to be very careful when you're looking at that because that used to fall under the all other peril deductible, but now oftent times to be more competitive, insurance companies will bump that up to 2% or 3% of your dwelling coverage. So, you think you're getting this great price, but if you have a claim, you're going to be out of pocket a lot more money. >> Right. They're they're treating the uh wind and hail deductible the same way they are a hurricane deductible. - What Are Essential Hurricane Supplies On A Budget? - Your Emergency Planner: Kind: captions Language: en What are essential hurricane supplies on a budget? Imagine preparing for a storm without emptying your wallet. When a hurricane is approaching, having the right supplies can keep you safe and comfortable, even on a tight budget. Let's go over some simple, cost-effective items you should gather. Now, first, water is your top priority. Store at least one gallon per person each day for at least 3 days. Use clean, reusable bottles like old juice containers to save money and cut down on plastic waste. Next, stock up on non-p perishable foods. Affordable options include canned vegetables, beans, tuna, peanut butter, rice, pasta, and energy bars. Buying in bulk or during sales helps keep costs low. Remember to include a manual can opener so you can access your food easily. For health and safety, assemble a basic first aid kit. Items like bandages, antiseptic wipes, pain relievers, scissors, and any prescribed medicines are essential. Many of these can be bought cheaply at dollar stores orarmacies. Lighting is also important. Get a small flashlight with extra batteries or a headlamp for hands-free use. Candles are risky because they can cause fires, so avoid relying on them. Pack some sturdy clothing and rain gear like ponchos or inexpensive rain jackets. Protect your feet with sturdy shoes to walk safely through debris. For sanitation, include soap, hand sanitizer, toilet paper, feminine hygiene products, and plastic trash bags. These lowcost items help keep you healthy during power outages. A multi-tool with a knife, pliers, and screwdrivers can be a lifesaver for small repairs. Keep copies of important documents and some cash in a waterproof container. Electronic devices might stop working, so paper backups are smart. Also, have matches or a lighter, plastic utensils, and a batterypowered radio to stay updated on weather alerts. To stay within your budget, buy a few items each week or during sales. Rotate your water and food supplies yearly to keep everything fresh. Focus on items your family already uses to avoid waste. Customize your kit to include needs for infants, elderly members, or pets, but keep it simple. Building your supplies gradually makes it easier to afford and less overwhelming. Having these essentials ready means you can face a hurricane with confidence, knowing you're prepared without overspending. Remember, preparedness is about being resourceful and staying safe during weather extremes and natural disasters. [Music]

  6. @lucybecker8 [1 likes]: I used to get sick-- dizzy, malaise, nausea, really miserable when flying. After 3 and a half hours I was done, was restless, miserable, frantic. The last hour on these shorter trips were awful and hard on my husband who watched me go through this and not able to help. I have motion sickness and thought it was just getting worse as I got older. What changed was once by chance I ate well before the flight and bought a lot of healthy food to take with. Now I make sure to eat regularly and well ( not snacks or candy or bready cakey stuff) and  keep drinking water, Evian was the best, really worth it. I eat well before boarding and make sure to buy a lot of the best food available at the ( overpriced) kiosks. Eating every hour and a half, or every couple of hours completely stopped the misery. I always have taken and still take ginger Gravol and wear acupuncture wristbands for motion sickness. Next flight I plan to take some sort of relaxant, either natural or a mild prescription med.
