- Nuclear Survival Day Nine—Tier Two Food, Radiation Math, and Staying Sane: Kind: captions Language: en Most people think the flash is the end. They think&nbsp; if the big one drops on a midsize town like yours,&nbsp;&nbsp; you just evaporate and that is that. They are&nbsp; wrong. The flash is the mercy. The real bill&nbsp;&nbsp; comes due on day nine. That is when the silence&nbsp; stops being eerie and starts feeling heavy.&nbsp;&nbsp; You were sitting in a basement or a reinforced&nbsp; hallway listening to the dust settle and you&nbsp;&nbsp; realize the world you knew did not just break, it&nbsp; was erased. Survival isn't about the blast. It is&nbsp;&nbsp; about the math of what is left behind. Go back to&nbsp; hour zero. You were probably at the grocery store&nbsp;&nbsp; or sitting in traffic on Fourth Street. Then the&nbsp; sky turned a color that does not exist in nature.&nbsp;&nbsp; It was not white. It was a searing predatory&nbsp; blue that burned right through your eyelids.&nbsp;&nbsp; You felt the heat before you heard the sound. That&nbsp; is the physics of it. Light travels faster than&nbsp;&nbsp; the shock wave. You had exactly 3 seconds&nbsp; to wonder if you were already dead before&nbsp;&nbsp; the pressure wave hammered the air out of your&nbsp; lungs and shattered every window in a 5m radius.&nbsp;&nbsp; The first 4 hours were pure chaos. People did&nbsp; not run to shelters. They ran into the streets&nbsp;&nbsp; screaming, looking for their kids, their spouses,&nbsp; anyone who could tell them this was not happening.&nbsp;&nbsp; You saw a guy trying to use his cell phone,&nbsp; staring at the dead screen like it was a&nbsp;&nbsp; holy relic that had failed him. Downrange, the&nbsp; horizon was a wall of black smoke and orange fire.&nbsp;&nbsp; The grid went down instantly. No&nbsp; sirens, no emergency broadcasts,&nbsp;&nbsp; just the sound of car alarms dying one by one as&nbsp; the electromagnetic pulse fried their electronics.&nbsp;&nbsp; This was your new SOP. Denial is the first&nbsp; thing that kills you in a gridown scenario.&nbsp;&nbsp; You saw it in the neighbors. They stayed in&nbsp; their living rooms with the windows blown out,&nbsp;&nbsp; waiting for the National Guard to roll down&nbsp; the street with bottled water and blankets.&nbsp;&nbsp; They thought this was a temporary glitch,&nbsp; like a bad storm. But you knew better.&nbsp;&nbsp; You saw the direction of the wind. You knew that&nbsp; black grit falling from the sky was not ash from&nbsp;&nbsp; a house fire. It was pulverized concrete and&nbsp; fallout. It was the clock ticking on your internal&nbsp;&nbsp; organs. You had to move. You dragged your family&nbsp; into the basement, not the center of the room,&nbsp;&nbsp; but the corner furthest below ground level. You&nbsp; grabbed the heavy oak table and tipped it over,&nbsp;&nbsp; piling every book, every gallon of water, and&nbsp; every suitcase you could find on top of it.&nbsp;&nbsp; Mass is your only friend when gamma rays&nbsp; are looking for a host. You were not just&nbsp;&nbsp; building a fort. You were building a leadlined&nbsp; coffin that you hoped would keep you alive.&nbsp;&nbsp; The kids were crying, asking why they could&nbsp; not turn on the lights. You had no answer.&nbsp;&nbsp; By hour three, the thirst set in. Not&nbsp; the kind of thirst you get after a jog.&nbsp;&nbsp; This was a metallic, dry heat in the back&nbsp; of your throat. You looked at the tap,&nbsp;&nbsp; but you did not touch it. You knew the city&nbsp; water towers were sitting ducks for fallout.&nbsp;&nbsp; Every drop in those pipes was a potential delivery&nbsp; system for iodine 131. Your daughter looked at you&nbsp;&nbsp; with cracked lips, holding her empty water bottle.&nbsp; That is when the guilt hits. You realize you spent&nbsp;&nbsp; years worrying about your 401k when you should&nbsp; have been worrying about 5gallon stackables.&nbsp;&nbsp; Outside the wind began to howl. It was a strange&nbsp; flat sound. There were no birds, no dogs barking,&nbsp;&nbsp; just the wind whipping through the&nbsp; skeletal remains of the neighborhood.&nbsp;&nbsp; You peeked through a crack in the cellar door and&nbsp; saw the sky had turned a sickly bruised purple.&nbsp;&nbsp; The fallout was coming down like heavy snow. But&nbsp; it didn't melt. It just piled up on the porch,&nbsp;&nbsp; a silent killer, waiting for a breeze to carry&nbsp; it into your lungs. You sealed the door with duct&nbsp;&nbsp; tape and prayed the seal held. That first night&nbsp; was the longest of your life. Every creek of the&nbsp;&nbsp; house sounded like a looter. Every cough from your&nbsp; son like the onset of radiation sickness. You sat&nbsp;&nbsp; there with a flashlight you knew would eventually&nbsp; run out of batteries, staring at the wall.&nbsp;&nbsp; You realized that the town was not coming to help.&nbsp; The police were at home with their own families.&nbsp;&nbsp; The fire department was overwhelmed. You&nbsp; were an island of four people in a sea of&nbsp;&nbsp; radioactive dust. Self-preservation had to shift&nbsp; to total protection. You were the only wall left.&nbsp;&nbsp; In the dark, you went over your inventory.&nbsp; Two cases of water, six cans of peaches,&nbsp;&nbsp; a bag of rice. You had no way to cook without&nbsp; making the basement smell like a target.&nbsp;&nbsp; You realized your gear was not a force multiplier&nbsp; because you did not have a force to multiply.&nbsp;&nbsp; You were just a guy with a backpack. If you&nbsp; wanted to survive to day 30, you needed a system.&nbsp;&nbsp; You needed to understand the physics of what&nbsp; was happening outside that basement door&nbsp;&nbsp; or you would be just another statistic by&nbsp; the end of the week. Looking at your wife&nbsp;&nbsp; sleeping on a cold concrete floor, you felt a&nbsp; new kind of fear. It was not the fear of dying.&nbsp;&nbsp; It was the fear of failing them. You realized&nbsp; that hope is not a strategy. It never was.&nbsp;&nbsp; If you had spent 10 minutes a week learning the&nbsp; actual protocols for a nuclear event, you would&nbsp;&nbsp; not be guessing right now. You would know exactly&nbsp; how many Renkins that basement war was blocking.&nbsp;&nbsp; But you didn't. You waited until the sky fell&nbsp; to start learning. Hope is not a strategy.&nbsp;&nbsp; Most people guess they have enough&nbsp; food, but the math says otherwise.&nbsp;&nbsp; When the grid goes dark, your intuition will&nbsp; fail you. You need hard data and a proven plan.&nbsp;&nbsp; Go to protocols survival.com right now.&nbsp; Calculate your survival score using our&nbsp;&nbsp; proprietary assessment tools. Do not wait&nbsp; for the flash to realize you are unprepared.&nbsp;&nbsp; Enroll in our free catastrophe survival 101 course&nbsp; and get the training before you actually need it.&nbsp;&nbsp; Your family's life depends on your competence,&nbsp; not your luck. Get the command center ready today.&nbsp;&nbsp; Welcome to day nine. This is the milestone that&nbsp; separates the survivors from the casualties.&nbsp;&nbsp; By now, the adrenaline has burned out. Your&nbsp; body is running on cortisol and stale crackers.&nbsp;&nbsp; The basement smells like unwashed skin&nbsp; and desperation. But something major has&nbsp;&nbsp; happened in the invisible world. Today marks the&nbsp; first halflife of iodine 131. Approximately half&nbsp;&nbsp; of that radioactive poison that blanketed your&nbsp; town on day one has decayed into stable zenon.&nbsp;&nbsp; It is a win that do not start celebrating yet.&nbsp; The invisible war is just changing its tactics.&nbsp;&nbsp; You are moving from the sprint to the marathon.&nbsp; Iodine 131 is the sprinter of isotopes. It hits&nbsp;&nbsp; hard and fast, targeting your thyroid and trying&nbsp; to cause permanent damage in the first week.&nbsp;&nbsp; But because its half-life is roughly 8 days,&nbsp; day 9 is the first time you can breathe a very&nbsp;&nbsp; small sigh of relief regarding your water and&nbsp; food safety. The hot iodine is diminishing.&nbsp;&nbsp; However, the ground outside is still a&nbsp; minefield of cesium 137 and strontium 90.&nbsp;&nbsp; Those isotopes do not care about a 9-day&nbsp; milestone. They are planning to stay&nbsp;&nbsp; for the next 30 years. Cesium 137 is the&nbsp; one that should keep you up at night.&nbsp;&nbsp; It is highly water soluble. It does not just&nbsp; sit on the ground. It moves with the rain,&nbsp;&nbsp; soaking into the soil and the muscle tissue&nbsp; of anything that eats from that soil.&nbsp;&nbsp; Strontium 90 is even more insidious.&nbsp; It looks like calcium to your body.&nbsp;&nbsp; If you ingest it, your bones will&nbsp; grab it and lock it in for decades.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is why day 9 is not an all clear. It is&nbsp; just a shift in the food consumption hierarchy.&nbsp;&nbsp; You have to be tactical about what&nbsp; you eat. Now, during the first week,&nbsp;&nbsp; you should have burned through your tier 1&nbsp; supplies. These are the pre-sealed packages,&nbsp;&nbsp; canned goods, and the stuff in the fridge&nbsp; that was still cold when the power died.&nbsp;&nbsp; Now, on day nine, you move to tier two. These are&nbsp; your pantry staples: rice, pasta, beans, flour.&nbsp;&nbsp; But here is the catch. They must have been&nbsp; stored in sealed containers away from the air.&nbsp;&nbsp; If that bag of flour was open on&nbsp; the counter when the fallout hit,&nbsp;&nbsp; it is no longer food. It is a delivery system&nbsp; for strontium. Be ruthless with your pantry.&nbsp;&nbsp; You might be tempted to look out the window and&nbsp; see the river. You might think about catching a&nbsp;&nbsp; fish to supplement your rations. Do not. Fish in&nbsp; contaminated water are bioaccumulation machines.&nbsp;&nbsp; The larger predatory fish have been eating smaller&nbsp; fish that have been eating contaminated algae.&nbsp;&nbsp; By the time it gets to your hook, that&nbsp; fish is a radioactive concentrate.&nbsp;&nbsp; The same goes for any fresh produce or&nbsp; dairy. If a cow ate contaminated grass,&nbsp;&nbsp; that milk is a poison cocktail. In the early&nbsp; weeks, if it grew outdoors, it is dead to you.&nbsp;&nbsp; There is a smell in the basement that&nbsp; is more dangerous than the radiation.&nbsp;&nbsp; It is the smell of human waste.&nbsp; Without functioning sewage systems,&nbsp;&nbsp; your toilet is now a biological hazard. You&nbsp; cannot just flush it and hope for the best.&nbsp;&nbsp; On day nine, sanitation becomes a life or death&nbsp; community priority. If you do not manage your&nbsp;&nbsp; waste, you will not die of radiation sickness. You&nbsp; will die of chalera or dysentery in a dark corner&nbsp;&nbsp; of your own home. This is where the carne nuclear&nbsp; war survival skills protocols become your bible.&nbsp;&nbsp; You need a system that doesn't involve your living&nbsp; space. If you have a yard, you need a covered pit&nbsp;&nbsp; latrine. It has to be as far from your shelter and&nbsp; your water source as practical. You dig it deep&nbsp;&nbsp; and you keep it covered to prevent flies from&nbsp; turning your waste into a neighborhood plague.&nbsp;&nbsp; If you are stuck inside, you use the two&nbsp; bucket system, one for liquids, one for solids.&nbsp;&nbsp; You use cat litter, sawdust, or even&nbsp; shredded newspapers to manage the moisture.&nbsp;&nbsp; You use lime or ash from a fireplace to treat the&nbsp; waste. It is disgusting. It is grueling. and it&nbsp;&nbsp; is absolutely mandatory for survival. Ventilation&nbsp; is the most overlooked requirement in a shelter.&nbsp;&nbsp; You are worried about the air coming in, but&nbsp; you should be worried about the air staying in.&nbsp;&nbsp; Without proper air flow, carbon dioxide levels&nbsp; rise, making you sluggish and making poor&nbsp;&nbsp; decisions. You need an improvised carne pump or&nbsp; at least a staggered vent system that lets air&nbsp;&nbsp; circulate without giving fallout a direct path to&nbsp; your lungs. Every breath you take in that basement&nbsp;&nbsp; is a calculation. You are balancing the risk of&nbsp; radiation against the certainty of suffocation.&nbsp;&nbsp; It is a grim math, but it is yours to solve.&nbsp; You might think this is overkill. You might&nbsp;&nbsp; think the National Guard or FEMA will roll in&nbsp; by day nine with chemical toilets and MREs.&nbsp;&nbsp; That is the common objection to doomsday&nbsp; prepping. But think about the scale.&nbsp;&nbsp; In a nuclear escalation, there is no outside&nbsp; help coming. The National Guard units in your&nbsp;&nbsp; town are made of people whose own homes were hit.&nbsp; Their families are in basement just like yours.&nbsp;&nbsp; The supply chains for fuel, food, and medicine&nbsp; are not just broken. They are vaporized.&nbsp;&nbsp; No one is coming to empty your bucket. You&nbsp; are the government now. Act accordingly.&nbsp;&nbsp; Day nine is when the psychological wall hits. The&nbsp; novelty of survival has worn off. The adventure&nbsp;&nbsp; is gone. Now it is just the cold reality of a&nbsp; world that is broken. You start to hear things.&nbsp;&nbsp; Maybe it is the wind. Or maybe it is a&nbsp; neighbor trying to get into your garage.&nbsp;&nbsp; Your nerves are frayed like an old&nbsp; rope. You have to maintain discipline.&nbsp;&nbsp; If you stop the sanitation SOP, if you&nbsp; start eating the tier three food too early,&nbsp;&nbsp; or if you leave the shelter because you just need&nbsp; some air, you are inviting death into your circle.&nbsp;&nbsp; On day nine, you might hear a knock. Not a loud&nbsp; one. A weak, desperate tapping on the basement&nbsp;&nbsp; window. It is the guy from three houses down. He&nbsp; did not prepare. He has two kids and no water.&nbsp;&nbsp; He is begging. This is the hardest part of the&nbsp; curriculum. If you open that door, you are not&nbsp;&nbsp; just sharing your water. You are letting in the&nbsp; fallout he is covered in. You are compromising the&nbsp;&nbsp; seal. You are choosing between your child's life&nbsp; and his. Guilt is a weight you will carry forever.&nbsp;&nbsp; But fear will keep you alive. You have to&nbsp; watch your family for the latent period.&nbsp;&nbsp; Many people who receive a lethal dose of&nbsp; radiation feel better after the first few days.&nbsp;&nbsp; It is a cruel trick of biology. By day nine, if&nbsp; anyone starts showing signs of recurring nausea,&nbsp;&nbsp; hair loss, or small purple bruises under the&nbsp; skin, you are looking at systemic failure.&nbsp;&nbsp; There is no hospital to go to. All you can&nbsp; do is keep them hydrated and comfortable.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is why you stay in the shelter.&nbsp; You are not just hiding from the rays.&nbsp;&nbsp; You are hiding from the heartbreak of seeing the&nbsp; fallout work. In the silence, you clean your gear.&nbsp;&nbsp; Your knife, your flashlights, your containers.&nbsp; Maintenance is a form of meditation.&nbsp;&nbsp; It keeps your hands busy and your mind sharp. If&nbsp; you let your tools rust, you are telling yourself&nbsp;&nbsp; that the future does not matter. But it does. You&nbsp; are cleaning that 10-in blade because on day 15,&nbsp;&nbsp; you are going to have to leave this basement.&nbsp; You are going to have to scout. And the world&nbsp;&nbsp; you step into will not be the one you left.&nbsp; It will be a scavenged, desperate landscape.&nbsp;&nbsp; You start calculating the calories again. You are&nbsp; at 1,200 a day for yourself, 1,500 for the kids.&nbsp;&nbsp; You are losing weight, but you're not starving&nbsp; yet. The rice is the backbone of your tier 2 plan.&nbsp;&nbsp; You have to figure out a way to soften it without&nbsp; using too much of your precious water. Maybe&nbsp;&nbsp; you soak it for 12 hours. Maybe you have a small&nbsp; butane stove, but you only use it for 3 minutes at&nbsp;&nbsp; a time to minimize the oxygen burn. Every meal is&nbsp; a tactical operation. Every calorie is a victory.&nbsp;&nbsp; The hardest thing to get used to is the silence.&nbsp; No humming from the refrigerator, no distant sound&nbsp;&nbsp; of the interstate, just the sound of your own&nbsp; heartbeat and the ragged breathing of your family.&nbsp;&nbsp; You realize how much of your life was built on&nbsp; the noise of the grid. Now that the grid is gone,&nbsp;&nbsp; you are left with the raw components of existence.&nbsp; Day nine is the day you stop being a consumer&nbsp;&nbsp; and start being a survivor. You are learning to&nbsp; live in the gaps. You are becoming something the&nbsp;&nbsp; old world wouldn't recognize. When the&nbsp; grid goes dark, the cloud goes with it.&nbsp;&nbsp; Your digital manuals, your bookmarked videos,&nbsp; and your saved maps will be gone in an instant.&nbsp;&nbsp; You need a command center that works offline,&nbsp; built into the hardware of your survival strategy.&nbsp;&nbsp; I built the protocol survival app to be that&nbsp; system. It allows you to secure your inventory,&nbsp;&nbsp; track your supplies, and access your free training&nbsp; modules even when there is no signal. Do not wait&nbsp;&nbsp; for the towers to fall. Secure your offline&nbsp; backup now while you still have a connection.&nbsp;&nbsp; protocols survival.com. Day 15. You have survived&nbsp; the most dangerous 2 weeks of the nuclear age.&nbsp;&nbsp; The iodine 131 is mostly gone, decayed&nbsp; into that stable xenon we talked about. The&nbsp;&nbsp; immediate gamma threat from the ground has dropped&nbsp; significantly, not because the isotopes are gone,&nbsp;&nbsp; but because they have settled and decayed.&nbsp; It is time to see what is left of your town.&nbsp;&nbsp; You put on your thickest clothes, your gloves,&nbsp; and your mask. You are not going for a stroll. You&nbsp;&nbsp; are going on a scouting mission. The goal, tier&nbsp; three food and a better sense of the perimeter.&nbsp;&nbsp; Walking out your front door is&nbsp; like stepping onto another planet.&nbsp;&nbsp; The neighborhood is a monochrome graveyard. The&nbsp; trees are gray, covered in a fine layer of dust.&nbsp;&nbsp; Your neighbor's car is still in the&nbsp; driveway, its tires melted or blown out.&nbsp;&nbsp; The silence is even louder&nbsp; here. You keep your head down.&nbsp;&nbsp; You are looking for hot spots, areas where&nbsp; the wind has piled up the fallout into drifts.&nbsp;&nbsp; You avoid the low ground where the rain might have&nbsp; concentrated the cesium. You move like a ghost.&nbsp;&nbsp; Your target is a local warehouse or a&nbsp; distribution center on the edge of town.&nbsp;&nbsp; You avoid the small convenience stores. They&nbsp; were looted or burned in the first 48 hours.&nbsp;&nbsp; You want the big industrial spaces.&nbsp; These are the locations of tier 3 food,&nbsp;&nbsp; sealed commercial inventories. You are looking&nbsp; for pallets of canned goods, bulk grains,&nbsp;&nbsp; and medical supplies that were protected&nbsp; by thick concrete walls and steel roofing.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is the fuel you need to get through&nbsp; day 30 and beyond. This is the mission.&nbsp;&nbsp; As you move, you are assessing the threats.&nbsp; It isn't just the radiation anymore.&nbsp;&nbsp; It is the other people who emerged. You see a&nbsp; group of three men near the old hardware store.&nbsp;&nbsp; They aren't looking for food.&nbsp; They are looking for power.&nbsp;&nbsp; You stay in the shadows. This is&nbsp; where your tactical training kicks in.&nbsp;&nbsp; You are not looking for a fight. A single scratch&nbsp; in this environment can lead to a fatal infection.&nbsp;&nbsp; You are a gatherer, not a warrior, but you carry&nbsp; your sidearm with a new kind of familiarity.&nbsp;&nbsp; The rule of law has been replaced by the rule&nbsp; of the prepared. You run into another survivor&nbsp;&nbsp; near the pharmacy. He looks like a skeleton. He is&nbsp; coughing a deep wet sound that tells you his lungs&nbsp;&nbsp; are full of fallout. He asks you for help. This&nbsp; is the test of your resolve. You have a choice.&nbsp;&nbsp; Help a dying man and risk your own health or keep&nbsp; moving. You remember your family in the basement.&nbsp;&nbsp; You remember the child with the cracked lips.&nbsp; You realize that you cannot save everyone.&nbsp;&nbsp; You can barely save yourself. You nod once and&nbsp; move on. The world has become a zero sum game.&nbsp;&nbsp; You reach the warehouse. The doors&nbsp; are heavy and the power is dead.&nbsp;&nbsp; You have to use a pry bar to get in. Inside, the&nbsp; air is cool and smells of cardboard and dust.&nbsp;&nbsp; It is a cathedral of supplies. This is the&nbsp; difference between surviving and thriving.&nbsp;&nbsp; You find a pallet of bottled water that hasn't&nbsp; been touched. You find cases of canned tuna.&nbsp;&nbsp; You feel a surge of hope, but you keep&nbsp; it in check. You can't carry a pallet.&nbsp;&nbsp; You have to be selective. You fill your pack&nbsp; with high protein, high calorie density items.&nbsp;&nbsp; The walk back is harder. Your pack is&nbsp; heavy and your legs feel like lead.&nbsp;&nbsp; You are constantly checking your back. You realize&nbsp; that your town has become a series of territories.&nbsp;&nbsp; Every street corner is a potential ambush. You&nbsp; take a back alley route through the skeletal&nbsp;&nbsp; remains of a park. You see the playground&nbsp; equipment rusted and covered in gray ash. It&nbsp;&nbsp; looks like a relic from a lost civilization. You&nbsp; realize that your kids will never play here again.&nbsp;&nbsp; The world didn't just break. It was replaced. When&nbsp; you get back to your shelter, the work isn't done.&nbsp;&nbsp; You cannot just walk in. You have a strict&nbsp; decontamination protocol. You strip off your&nbsp;&nbsp; outer layers in the garage. You wipe yourself down&nbsp; with a damp cloth, careful not to spread the dust.&nbsp;&nbsp; You dispose of the cloth in a sealed bag. Only&nbsp; then do you enter the basement. Your wife looks&nbsp;&nbsp; at you with wide eyes. You drop the bag of food&nbsp; on the table. It is the first real win in 2 weeks.&nbsp;&nbsp; But the price of that win was the realization&nbsp; of how dangerous the outside world has become.&nbsp;&nbsp; You spend the evening looking at your maps.&nbsp; You have to plan your next moves around the&nbsp;&nbsp; cesium 137. You need to find a way to&nbsp; get more water that isn't contaminated.&nbsp;&nbsp; You start thinking about rain catchment systems&nbsp; with sand and charcoal filters. You are no longer&nbsp;&nbsp; just reacting to the blast. You are building a new&nbsp; life in the ruins. You are thinking about months,&nbsp;&nbsp; not days. You are thinking about how to keep your&nbsp; family sane when the world outside is a graveyard.&nbsp;&nbsp; You are the architect of their future. By&nbsp; day 30, the realization fully sinks in.&nbsp;&nbsp; The grid is not coming back. You see a plane&nbsp; in the distance high up, but it doesn't land.&nbsp;&nbsp; It is probably a reconnaissance flight from some&nbsp; part of the country that wasn't hit as hard.&nbsp;&nbsp; But they aren't dropping supplies. They&nbsp; are just watching. You are on your own.&nbsp;&nbsp; The midsize town you lived in&nbsp; is now a survival laboratory.&nbsp;&nbsp; You have passed the first month, but the challenge&nbsp; is changing. You are moving into the long hall.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is where the true character of a survivor&nbsp; is forged. Welcome to day 31. You have outlived&nbsp;&nbsp; 80% of your neighbors. The ones who are left&nbsp; are either like you prepared and disciplined&nbsp;&nbsp; or they are the predators who survived by taking&nbsp; from others. The social collapse is complete.&nbsp;&nbsp; There are no more nice people. There are only&nbsp; those who have something and those who want it.&nbsp;&nbsp; Your world has shrunk to the size of your&nbsp; house and the two blocks surrounding it.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is the long hall where the real battle&nbsp; for survival shifts from physics to psychology. The rules of society have evaporated. In the old&nbsp; world, if you are hungry, you went to the store.&nbsp;&nbsp; In this world, if you are hungry, you look for&nbsp; a weakness. You see it in the way people move.&nbsp;&nbsp; Quick, nervous, always looking over their&nbsp; shoulders. You have to establish a perimeter.&nbsp;&nbsp; You and the two families you trust&nbsp; have formed a small security detail.&nbsp;&nbsp; You watch each other's backs while someone&nbsp; scavengers or works the filters. Trust is the&nbsp;&nbsp; most valuable currency you have left, and it is in&nbsp; very short supply. Money is paper. Gold is heavy.&nbsp;&nbsp; On day 40, you realize that the real B items&nbsp; are salt, antibiotics, and clean water.&nbsp;&nbsp; You trade a spare flashlight for a bottle of a&nbsp; moxicylin. It is a high price, but your daughter&nbsp;&nbsp; has a fever and a cut that won't heal. You are&nbsp; making deals in the shadows of burnedout houses.&nbsp;&nbsp; You realize that the economy is just a way to&nbsp; quantify desperation. Every trade is a risk.&nbsp;&nbsp; You never show your full hand. You never let them&nbsp; know how much you actually have back at the house.&nbsp;&nbsp; Even with your tier three scavenging, the food is&nbsp; running low. The starvation is not a sudden thing.&nbsp;&nbsp; It is a slow grinding ache in your&nbsp; stomach. Your muscles start to waste away.&nbsp;&nbsp; You become irritable. Your judgment clouded by&nbsp; the lack of nutrients. You have to be disciplined.&nbsp;&nbsp; You weigh every portion. You eat the&nbsp; things you hate because they have calories.&nbsp;&nbsp; You realize that a human body can go a long time&nbsp; without food, but the mind breaks much faster.&nbsp;&nbsp; You have to keep the morale up,&nbsp; even when the bowl is half empty.&nbsp;&nbsp; As the days turn into months,&nbsp; the weather becomes a new enemy.&nbsp;&nbsp; Without the urban heat island effect and&nbsp; the heating systems of a modern city,&nbsp;&nbsp; the cold is brutal. You are huddled around a small&nbsp; wood stove you improvised from a 55gall drum.&nbsp;&nbsp; You are burning the furniture to stay warm. You&nbsp; realize that survival is a series of tradeoffs.&nbsp;&nbsp; You burn the dining table to keep the kids&nbsp; from freezing, but that means you are losing&nbsp;&nbsp; a piece of your former life. The house is&nbsp; becoming a shell just like the world outside.&nbsp;&nbsp; There are no dentists. There are no&nbsp; doctors. A simple tooth infection can become&nbsp;&nbsp; a death sentence. You spend your evenings&nbsp; reading the medical manuals you scavenged,&nbsp;&nbsp; learning how to stitch wounds and treat&nbsp; infections with the meager supplies you have.&nbsp;&nbsp; You realize how much you took for granted. The&nbsp; simple ability to get a shot of lidocaine is a&nbsp;&nbsp; fantasy. Now you are practicing 19th century&nbsp; medicine with a 21st century knowledge base.&nbsp;&nbsp; And the gap between the two is where the fear&nbsp; lives. You pray no one gets seriously ill.&nbsp;&nbsp; You walk past the house of the neighbor&nbsp; who begged for water on day nine.&nbsp;&nbsp; The windows are broken and the door is&nbsp; hanging off its hinges. They didn't make it.&nbsp;&nbsp; You feel a pang of guilt, but it is quickly&nbsp; replaced by a cold sense of validation.&nbsp;&nbsp; If you had helped them, you&nbsp; might be in that house with them.&nbsp;&nbsp; Survival is not about being a good&nbsp; person. It is about being a living person.&nbsp;&nbsp; You have to harden your heart to the&nbsp; suffering of those who didn't listen.&nbsp;&nbsp; It is the only way to keep your own family&nbsp; safe. By day 90, you have a routine.&nbsp;&nbsp; You wake up, check the perimeter, check the&nbsp; water filters, and plan the day's forage.&nbsp;&nbsp; You don't think about the old world much anymore.&nbsp; It feels like a movie you saw a long time ago.&nbsp;&nbsp; Your kids don't ask about the internet or the&nbsp; TV. They ask if the traps caught anything.&nbsp;&nbsp; They have become scavengers too. You see the&nbsp; hardness in their eyes and it breaks your heart.&nbsp;&nbsp; But you also know that hardness is what&nbsp; will keep them alive in this new world.&nbsp;&nbsp; They are the first generation of the&nbsp; ruins. You hear a rumor from a traveler,&nbsp;&nbsp; a guy passing through who traded some news for&nbsp; a meal. He says there are organized communities&nbsp;&nbsp; three counties over. They have a working&nbsp; well and a garden. They are building a wall.&nbsp;&nbsp; It sounds like a dream, but you have to decide.&nbsp; Do you stay here in the safety of your cellar,&nbsp;&nbsp; or do you risk the open road for&nbsp; the chance of something better?&nbsp;&nbsp; This is the ultimate survival dilemma. Staying&nbsp; is certain decay. Leaving is uncertain danger.&nbsp;&nbsp; You look at your map and your dwindling&nbsp; supplies. The decision has to be made soon.&nbsp;&nbsp; As you sit in the quiet of day 100, you realize&nbsp; you have changed. You are not the person who&nbsp;&nbsp; panicked in the grocery store traffic.&nbsp; You are leaner, tougher, and more aware.&nbsp;&nbsp; You have learned that human beings are incredibly&nbsp; resilient, but only if they have a plan. You have&nbsp;&nbsp; turned a nightmare into a manageable reality.&nbsp; You are still here. Your family is still here.&nbsp;&nbsp; You have defied the odds of the nuclear age. You&nbsp; are the proof that preparation is not paranoia.&nbsp;&nbsp; It is the foundation of the future. We spend&nbsp; our lives worrying about the wrong things.&nbsp;&nbsp; We worry about the stock market, the Wi-Fi speed,&nbsp; and the opinions of people we don't even like.&nbsp;&nbsp; But when the sky turns blue and the&nbsp; grid goes down, all of that evaporates.&nbsp;&nbsp; You were left with the only things that ever&nbsp; truly mattered. The health of your family and the&nbsp;&nbsp; knowledge in your head. The grid isn't what keeps&nbsp; the lights on. It is what keeps the monsters out.&nbsp;&nbsp; And once the lights are gone, you find out very&nbsp; quickly which one you are. Are you the victim&nbsp;&nbsp; or the protector? Day nine was the milestone of&nbsp; the isotope, but every day after is a milestone of&nbsp;&nbsp; your will. Do not wait for the flash to decide who&nbsp; you are going to be. Start the training now. Get&nbsp;&nbsp; the systems in place. Secure your family's future&nbsp; before the world decides to take it from you.&nbsp;&nbsp; Go to protocols survival.com. Get the app,&nbsp; take the course, and build your command center.&nbsp;&nbsp; Because when the silence comes, the&nbsp; only thing you will have to rely on&nbsp;&nbsp; is the preparation you did today. The&nbsp; clock is ticking. Stay alert. Stay safe.

The cannon loadout order is key. Your frigate fires the secondary battery first and then the main battery (which also has more cannons) so you want to devastate with the first round, lowering their defenses by 30% and your second round being even bigger guns boosted with 40% damage can more often than not disable or sink a ship on your first pass. It’s insanely effective.

- Ergonomics Expert Explains How to Sleep on a Plane | WSJ Pro Perfected: Kind: captions Language: en - [Narrator] Sleeping on a plane is hard, especially if you're in an economy seat. So we asked an expert for advice. - I'm an Occupational Therapist, Industrial Designer, and Certified Professional Ergonomist. With seated-sleep posture, not only do you fall into these awkward postures, but you're also compressing the vertebrae and your spine at the different levels. You have to plan ahead. - [Narrator] Here's what a professional ergonomist recommends for sleeping in an economy plane seat. First, you might consider using the tray table, but. - The position when using the tray table, if you're propping up on your forearms, that awkward positioning, the contact pressure against the tray table, all that's gonna irritate tissue and sleep just like it would during your waking hours. - [Narrator] Contact pressure happens when soft tissues are pressed against a hard surface for too long, slowing blood flow to your extremities, and often causing numbness or tingling. - With soft tissue compression, you can compromise circulation. You can impinge nerves, which can lead to numbness or tingling, which will often wake people up. - [Narrator] Then there's your posture. - [Carla] It's going to be difficult for you to maintain that posture very long because you will be experiencing excessive flexion at your hips and knees. Your spine will be rounded. You'll be putting pressure through your forearms. - [Narrator] The solve? - There are both inflatable and memory foam cushions available that start to at least address some of those risk factors, such as the contact pressure against the hard surface of the tray table by providing a soft surface for your body to rest. They can hold your arms and then it also cradles your head and neck and your face in a way that attempts to at least position your head and neck in some sort of a neutral posture. - [Narrator] But depending on your height, and how far back the person in front of you reclines, the tray table might not be your best option. - I think it's probably gonna serve you well just for sort of a short stretch for a nap. I don't think you'll be getting the highest quality of sleep in that position. - [Narrator] So what about leaning back in your seat? - If you have to sleep in an economy class seat in a seated position, you are up against some challenges. Ideal state, your body would would prefer to sleep in a fully-supported lying down position. Your body already has to endure weightbearing and force through your spine, your hips, your pelvis, your lower extremities. Whenever it's sleeping, ideally, it is great if you can steal a couple degrees of recline from your seatback just to put your body in a little bit more of a supported sleep position. - [Narrator] Just be mindful of your neighbors. You can also adjust your back position. - Putting just a gentle cushion behind the seatback to support the spine is beneficial. The more head and neck support you can provide, the better chance you have at achieving some restful sleep. - [Narrator] And here's where a travel pillow comes in handy. - A lot of these supports are not all created equal. There are the tried and true U-shaped neck pillows. You wanna keep your head and neck as neutral as possible against the unfortunate barrier of gravity and upright positioning. A lot of the ones that I like wrap around the neck and have a support just so they don't shift during sleep. They can sort of provide full neck support, so not just forward flexion, but lateral flexion, even some extension. It can hold your head in like a comfortable, yet supported position for sleep. - [Narrator] And if you don't have a pillow, as a last resort. - You can try to make do with layers of clothing. I think the thesis here is that if you are in a scenario where you have to sleep or you're flying during evening hours, you have to plan ahead. - [Narrator] Besides pillows and posture, there are a few other helpful tips for sleeping on planes. - You might think about limiting light exposure with an eye mask, or having layers or a blanket for thermal comfort, noise-canceling headphones just to limit some of the auditory stimulation on a plane. - [Narrator] When it comes to picking between the aisle, middle, and window seats, there isn't one best option for everyone. - It's gonna be a bit subjective in terms of what people find most comfortable sleeping on an airplane. - [Narrator] If you need to use the bathroom or stretch frequently, the aisle might be your best option, but it also means you'll be woken up every time your seatmates need to get out as well. - I think if you can avoid the middle seat, that's probably the best advice. - [Narrator] And while the window seat can provide support, just make sure to add a pillow or piece of clothing as a cushion. Importantly, flyers should find a position that allows them to move around a bit while they sleep, like they normally would in bed. - The body is built to be dynamic, and your body needs those movement breaks in order to lubricate joints, to release muscle tension, to mitigate strain. - [Narrator] But for those people who still really can't snooze mid-flight. - It's always worth it to get an upgrade. (bright music)

- Which insurance is better for old car? | Can a 15 year old car get comprehensive insurance? | Groww: Kind: captions Language: en Are you wondering whether your old car needs insurance? You're not alone. This question comes to many people's minds. - How to Choose Curtains for Home | Fabric, Color & Type Explained 🪄: Kind: captions Language: en If you still install such flower and leaf curtains in your house, I will set them on fire. In this video I am going to share types of curtains, installation process, measurements and fabric. But not as a joke, literally. If we differentiate curtains only on the basis of their optics, that is, how much light can enter the room after installing it, then we have a total of four options. First comes the Share Curtain. After installing this, maximum natural light comes inside the room. That means almost 80%. Light filtering curtains are slightly denser than this. Only up to 70% sunlight can pass through it. The third is room darkening curtain, which allows very little light to enter the room, i.e. up to 20 to 30%. And fourth is blackout curtains. Through this, maximum only 10 to 15% light can pass. Now, after hearing about blackout curtains, don't assume that these are simple curtains. You can also customize or directly purchase curtains with such patterns. Now many of you might be thinking that blackout curtains do not allow any sunlight to enter the room. Why install curtains that do not give you any natural light? Yes true. But actually, harsh sunlight can cause your wooden flooring or furniture to fade in the long term. That is why it is important to use blackout curtains. Especially if your space has such fully glazed windows or south facing windows because they allow maximum direct sunlight. Now sometimes along with blackout curtains, some natural light is also needed. Now in such cases we use sheer [music] curtains. It is used with blackout curtains. This will provide protection from UV rays and also provide natural light. Now, apart from traditional curtains, if you are wondering what would be the best treatment for a small window, then I would suggest you can go with roller blinds or Roman curtains. This will maintain the overall look of your space and also give you the best treatment for small windows. Now let us understand the different types of curtains. In which the first is grommet curtains which have two types. First is the eyelid. [MUSIC] This is the most common type. In this, ring is provided in such courts. The second is pleated curtains that are hung on tracks. There are many types of plated curtains. Such as Taylor pleat, pinch pleat, inverted, goblet and French [MUSIC] pleat. The most popular choice among all these is three pleat curtains. Now the second type is double panel curtains. Just like I recommended you. You can pair and use blackout curtains and sheer curtains together. So now you will understand how to pair it. In this you have to set both the curtains in two different layers. With this you can adjust both the curtains at one time according to your need. In this, the first visible layer outward i.e. in the room will be black out curtains. And in its second layer, sheer curtain will be used on the outside. It can be accessed like this. If you have any confusion regarding curtains or any query related to layout, material or colour combination in your space, you can ask me on a one to one video call. Where I will myself clear all your queries. And you will find the link to book a consultation in the description. Which color should be selected for curtains ? This is the most common question. So, as I told you in this color wheel video , how can you select the right color for your space ? Which I will explain here in short. You can select three colors in a room's color palette. One that will be used in 60% of the area of the entire room. Secondary color which occupies 30% area. [Music] And third is the accent color that you can use in decor. Now for curts you can select the primary or secondary color of your space. Try to go mostly with neutrals like beige, [music] cream, shades of grey or brown or you can choose some darker colours in digital prints which will be your accent [music] colour. Now it is also important to select the best fabric for the kurtas. Because curtains occupy almost a full wall in your space. That's why both maintenance and [musical] look have to be kept in mind while selecting its fabric. The first option is silk. This fabric is very rich. And it looks perfect in all rooms. The second evergreen option is cotton. Although it is quite easy to maintain it. But if the curtain is of lighter color then finger stains can be visible on it. Third is the most durable and easy to maintain polyester. It is the easiest to clean and maintain. Fourth, velvet is a luxurious looking fabric but it is very high maintenance and its look is also very heavy. So if you are not achieving a high end glamour look at all then you do not need it. The fifth fabric is linen. It looks the most light and airy. But linen is also an expensive material [MUSIC] with high maintenance. This is a perfect choice for both themes. And the sixth [music] fabric is the jacket which again gives a very classy look. This also requires some maintenance. But it's a great choice for Kurts. While installing curtains, always extend the curtain rod by 6 inches on both sides so that you get proper view and sunlight. If there is a full length window, then take the curtains only up to the height of the skirting so that the curtains do not get damaged from the bottom during cleaning. Now you might be wondering whether it is necessary to take such payments for curtains? Look, it's not necessary. But if you are going to buy Rhodes kurtas then it would be better to consider this. Because with this you will be able to hide the top part of the curtains. And if you are not adding pelmets at all, then you can hang curtains with such channels from the ceiling. These look very beautiful and add volume to your space. You can buy curtains online from IKYA, Drape Story, Neede, Amazon, Flipkart, Urban Leader, Cotton Culture and Facebook India. - Tips for Choosing Your Roman Shade: Kind: captions Language: en add Uptown drapes we want the Romans that you choose to be customized for your very own Windows e fabric is hand-picked and every window span is specifically measured to meet your very own needs but along with the fabric Choice it's also really important to decide how your Roman is going to be hung and installed in your space there's actually four different ways that you can hang your Roman you can do an outside Mount as a waterfall style and also an inside mount as a waterfall style there's also an outside mount with a front facing balance and an inside mount with a front facing balance let's say your window opens up into your room or you don't want to block your view or you just want your window to appear taller than it actually is we would recommend using an outside Mount and mounting it above your window and that way your window can freely open up into the room if your window has enough depth and you can mount your Roman inside and it lays flush or just comes out a little bit then an inside mount would be a good choice for that situation if you have French doors and you're mounting your Romans on the actual door a front-facing balance is going to prevent your Roman from having big side gaps these are just some tips to consider when you're ordering your own Roman Shades But ultimately the decision is yours so make it custom with Uptown drapes

- Curtain Design For Home Interiors | Curtain Types | Blackout & Sheer Curtains | What To Choose When?: Kind: captions Language: en you are watching this video then you must be confused whether to go for grommet curtains or double panel Roman blinds or Venetian blinds. I know that sometimes you need sunlight in the room and sometimes a complete blackout, so which curtain will be best for you? Whatever doubts you have regarding these curtains will be cleared by this video and yes, see this list a little above. First of all let's talk about curtain opacity which has four types - Blackout Room Darkening, Light Filtering and Sheer Blackout Curtain provides maximum protection and as its name suggests, it will make the room completely dark. In Complete Blackout Room Darkening, only 10 to 15% light can pass through the curtain. In light filtering, more light than both Blackout and Room Darkening will pass through the curtain and Sheer Curtain will allow maximum light to pass through the curtain. Out of these four curtain opacities, Blackout Curtain and Sheer Curtain are used the most, so let me explain in detail where and how to use these two. It's crucial to understand the importance of blackout curtains, especially for modern homes. They control an average of 95 to 100% of external light, making them a perfect solution for bedrooms, media rooms, and nurseries. If the fabric is thick, you can use it directly as a main curtain. If your fabric is a little thinner, use it with a backing. This helps reduce the load on your AC or heater by controlling heat and cold. Thick fabric also minimizes outside noise, making the room peaceful. If your window faces south, there's maximum sunlight. In such conditions, curtains protect your furniture, flooring, and fabrics from fading due to harmful UV rays. I've experienced this myself. In my own bedroom, a blackout curtain that wasn't allowing 100% of the light to pass through faded within five to six years, allowing only 20 to 25% of the light to pass through. Now, imagine if a blackout curtain could be in this condition. If this weren't the case, how much damage would the direct sun rays cause to my wooden flooring and furniture? So, if your window faces south, blackout curtains are mandatory. These curtains come in multiple options in many colors and patterns. You can also get a customized design according to the theme of your room. The average cost of blackout curtains starts from ₹3300 per meter. Nowadays, it often happens that someone's house or office is in front of your window exit, due to which your privacy gets compromised. If you use only blackout curtains, the house will remain dark the whole day. In such cases, you can use sheer curtains. Its biggest advantage is that it gives you natural light as well as vision. It is a curtain made of translucent fabric and net-like material, which is mostly made of polyester, cotton, and linen fabric. Sheer curtains are available in many different colors and patterns. You can choose printed, textured and printed curtains according to your theme. You can buy embroidered curtains. Major areas in India experience extreme sunlight for six to seven hours, so while selecting your curtains, one thing you must keep in mind is that the fabric should be durable. The average cost of sheer curtains is Rs. 10000 per meter. Now you will ask that there is a south facing window, we need privacy and some sunlight in the house, so what should we do? You can use sheer curtains in combination with blackout curtains. Let me tell you how, but before that, one important thing, if you also have any doubts, big or small, while getting your house interior done, then you can connect with me on video call from any corner of the world. I will clear your queries myself. You will find the link for this in the description. Now that you have come to know about curtain opacity, let's talk about different types of curtains. First is groomed curtains. This is the most popular type which is best for living room and bedrooms. There are two types of curtains: eyelet, in which there will be a ring on top for the rod. These rings slide easily through the curtain rod, allowing them to open and close smoothly. The second type of grommet curtains are pleated curtains, which are hung on tracks. Pleated curtains also have many different options, such as tailored pleat, pinch pleat, inverted pleat, goblet, fringe, extra. The most popular style is the three-pleat curtain, which gives a very elegant look. Second are double panel curtains. Your question was how to use both blackout curtains and sheer curtains together. This is the solution to this. The outer layer will have a blackout curtain and the inner layer will have a sheer curtain. You can use it according to your need, whether you want natural light or blackout. If you are planning double panel curtains, you will have to purchase a track for it in advance. Third are Roman blinds. These look elegant and are the best option for blackout, but the only problem is whether they will remain completely open or half closed. Even if kept open, privacy will be compromised. Fourth is roller blinds, like Roman blinds. When opened, layers are created. In roller blinds, the entire curtain will roll onto the track. These are mostly used. Fifth is Venetian blinds. These are also commonly used in offices, which will also maintain privacy if needed. Sixth is rod pocket curtains. These are a bit old, but if you have a rented room and don't want to spend too much, then go for it. While installing curtains, remember these three things: choose curtains six inches larger than the window size on both sides so that your view isn't blocked whenever you open the curtain. If it's a full-glazed window, choose curtains up to the height of the skirting so that they do n't get damaged during daily mopping. And last is choosing curtains 10 inches larger than the window height. All the curtain types and styles we've discussed will look beautiful with pelmets. Now, how do you create this pelmet if you have to install both the main curtain and the sheer curtain? So keep a space of 8 to 10 inches in the pelmet, 6 to 8 inches for Roman blinds, 5 to 6 inches for roller blinds and 4 inches for Venetian single curtains. - Hurricanes 101 | National Geographic: Kind: captions Language: en (heavy winds blowing) - [Narrator] Cyclone, typhoon, hurricane. All of these names are used around the world to describe the most powerful storm known to man. Hurricanes are unpredictable but scientists have a through understanding of how hurricanes form and sustain their power. In the Atlantic Ocean, hurricane season peaks during the late summer months when tropical waters are the warmest. Hurricanes form from a cluster of thunderstorms that suck up the warm, moist air and move it high into Earth's atmosphere. The warm air is then converted into energy that powers the hurricane's circular winds. These winds spin around a low-pressure center called the eye. Which can provide a 20 to 30 mile radius of eerie calm. Encircling it is the eye wall, a towering ring of clouds with some of the fastest wind-speeds of the hurricane. Surrounding the eye wall are curved bands of clouds. The rainbands, often tens of miles wide releasing sheets of rain and sometimes tornadoes. When a tropical storm's winds reach at least 74 miles per hour, it becomes a hurricane. The hurricane then receives the category ranking of one to five on the Saffir-Simpson Scale based on its wind speed and potential damage. But wind speed isn't always the most dangerous component when hurricanes come near land. It's storm surge. Storm surge is caused when winds from an approaching hurricane push water towards the shoreline up to 20 feet above sea level and can extend 100 miles. 90% of all hurricane deaths are the result of storm surge. While hurricanes can cause mass devastation, just like other natural disasters, they serve a higher purpose within the global ecosystem. Hurricanes help regulate our climate by moving heat energy from the equator to the poles keeping the Earth's temperature stable. Over time, science has helped us to better understand hurricanes and predict their paths. Saving lives through early warning systems and helping us build better infrastructure to protect our cities. The more we study these complex storms, the better we can prepare for them and minimize their impact on human lives.

- Hurricane, Tornado, Cyclone – What’s the Difference?: Kind: captions Language: en Aaah! Take cover! It’s a hurricane! Or is it a tornado? Or maybe a typhoon? How the heck do you tell the difference between all these things?! Well, fear not, because I have it covered for you right here. First, let’s get it straight: all these weather phenomena have to do with strong winds. Not my-umbrella-got-torn-from-my-hands strong, mind you, but rather my-house-got-blown-away-to-the-Land-of-Oz kind of thing. Yet before we get to the actual difference between hurricanes and typhoons, we must understand that, in general, they’re one and the same thing called a tropical cyclone. A tropical cyclone is a huge mass of clouds that gather in the sky and start rotating counter clock-wise due to very low air pressure. After a while it lures in moisture from all over the place, becoming bigger and more dangerous. This rotation may or may not become a trouble, but its main quality that never changes is that cyclones always form over the oceans or seas. Also, if you live in moderate or colder climates, there’s nothing for you to worry about, even if your house is right by the seaside, because you’ll only hear about cyclones from the news. After all, they’re called tropical for a reason: they only form in tropical or subtropical areas, because they need warm water to get things going. Now, if they’re all the same, why have different names for them? The short answer is to confuse everyone. I guess you’ll need a longer one after this. So, like I said, tropical cyclones may even not pose any serious threat to people. In fact, cyclones occur much more often than you think! But only few of them grow large and strong enough to become hurricanes or typhoons. The weakest version of a tropical cyclone is called a tropical depression. It’s a similar swirling mass of clouds, usually accompanied by storms, but the force of winds in it is not very high. The strongest gusts are 39 mph, which is a lot, I know, but it’s rarely the case. On dry land, such weather would only require you to close all windows and doors and stay at home. You can even watch the raging storm while sitting on a windowsill with a cup of hot chocolate. Still, if you’re caught by a depression in the open sea, it may and will be a problem because the force of wind is enough to overturn a boat, and a heavy rain with lightning doesn’t help the situation. When the winds are stronger than 39 mph, the cyclone is then called a tropical storm. This is a more serious threat, and you’ll do well to hide inside your house because gusts of wind might reach 72 mph. Imagine a car driving at full speed on a highway — that will be the force of wind during a severe tropical storm for you. And only if the wind gets even more powerful than that, then it can be called a hurricane or a typhoon. And here’s where we get to the difference between the two: in fact, there’s almost none! If a severe tropical cyclone with wind speeds of over 75 mph occurs in the North Atlantic or North-East Pacific, it’s called a hurricane. If it’s in the North-West Pacific, then it’s a typhoon. All the rest about them is the same, and a hurricane can even become a typhoon, or vice versa, if it travels from one area to the other. We hear more about hurricanes than typhoons because the Atlantic Ocean is warmer than the Pacific, and warm water acts like a fuel for cyclones. It evaporates from the surface and then condenses again in the cold air, forming clouds, which clump together and start swirling around with the wind. This way, cyclones can cover extremely large areas and transform into real monsters under right conditions. But what about tornadoes? Are they the same as cyclones? Um, no. In fact, these two can’t be more different. First of all, tropical cyclones only form in warm areas over the ocean; they can never occur anywhere else. Tornadoes, on the contrary, can form almost anywhere they please, both over sea and over land. But there’s much more to it than that. You see, a tornado is a swirling funnel of air coming down from the sky. It appears during thunderstorms, and it’s rather a consequence than a reason for severe weather. The column of air falls down to the ground, and wind speeds there can reach anything from 100 to the whopping 300 mph. Needless to say, anything that gets in its way will be hurled upwards, be it a person, a car, or Dorothy’s house. But despite its terrible power, a tornado is a very local event, and short-lived at that. The biggest one ever registered by scientists was just over 1.5 mi in diameter and lasted about an hour or so. Compared to tropical cyclones, which can stretch over 1,000 mi and last for days or even weeks, it’s like a grain of sand on a beach. By the way, cyclones coming to the shore can also be accompanied by several tornadoes forming over land. So they’re something like sidekicks to the big bad boys. Still, tornadoes and hurricanes (or typhoons, if you prefer the northwestern Pacific) are not entirely different, after all, and they have certain similarities too. Remember the swirling part? That’s the thing. Both hurricanes and tornadoes are powerful masses of air rotating around the center at high speeds. And the center, in its turn, is usually calm and windless — so calm, in fact, that it’s almost creepy. It’s called the eye of the tornado or the hurricane, and it’s basically the safest place to be when the phenomenon comes to you. Well, apart from being thousands of miles away from it, of course. Unfortunately, you’ll have to be really lucky to get into the eye and wait until the weather calms down. The eyewall has the strongest winds, and if you get too close to it, you’ll probably be thrown around like a toy. Also, if you’re at sea, then the eye is not safe at all. The winds all around the eye make huge waves crisscrossing the area inside, and your ship or boat will have a really hard time staying afloat. Not to mention that only the eye of a hurricane is safe on land; tornadoes are much smaller in diameter, and you can easily get in a lot of trouble if you’re caught into the eye of one by some wild chance. Have you ever seen the eye of a hurricane, by the way? Share your experience down in the comments! If you’ve been wondering how exactly powerful a hurricane could be, then you should know there are 5 categories of hurricanes according to the National Weather Service. Category 1 is just slightly more intense than a tropical storm, and in some countries it’s still considered as such. The wind speeds can reach 94 mph, which is a lot of force to deal with, but it’s not as if you would not be safe behind concrete walls of an apartment building. Category 2 is another story altogether. The wind blows at speeds of up to 109 mph, and that’s where the real trouble starts. The gusts are so strong they can fell trees and billboards in the streets, so it becomes really dangerous outside. Luckily, you’re still okay if you’re inside a sturdy building and away from the windows. When a hurricane grows to Category 3, it’s already a disaster. First of all, it’s big. Secondly, it’s powerful. The winds are reaching 129 mph, and catching such a gust is like being hit with a race car. That’s why this kind of a hurricane is called a major one. Not to mention that the rain and lightning add to the atmosphere of a cataclysm. It’s best to hide in a cellar or some other reinforced place and wait until the worst is over. A Category 4 major hurricane is something you don’t want to see with your own eyes. With gusts of wind up to 157 mph, it can tear trees from the ground and hurl fairly large objects in the air, causing lots of damage. The most recent Category 4 hurricane occurred in August-September of 2018, and it was named Florence. It traveled from off the western coast of Africa, where it began as a tropical depression, and grew in size and force until it became one of the most powerful hurricanes of the recent years. And finally, a Category 5 major hurricane is a thing to be avoided at all costs. In other classifications it’s even called a super typhoon or a super cyclonic storm, which says a lot. The most chilling thing about it is, of course, the strength of wind, and the most powerful super cyclone in the recorded history was hurricane Allen that struck the Caribbean in July-August of 1980. The speed of wind was the incredible 190 mph! It’s like a bullet train made of air and blowing continuously over a huge area! Well now, what’s that thing with the names? In fact, they’re not only given to hurricanes, but to all tropical cyclones that reach wind speeds of over 40 mph. And it’s not done for fun either: cyclones usually appear at specific seasons, and there can be more than one of them at once, moving in different directions. So weather services around the world give them names not to confuse them with each other. One other thing: some time ago, they only named hurricanes after women. Anyway, a few years ago, equal opportunity came to the weather service, and now tropical storms are named after both genders. Hey, if you learned something new today, then give the video a like and share it with a friend! And here are some other videos I think you'll enjoy. Just click to the left or right, and stay on the Bright Side of life!

- Types of precipitation/Different forms of precipitation/ Rain, hail, snow, sleet: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] we all enjoy rain but have you wonder how this water falls on the earth and in and the conditions of the air between the clouds and the ground precipitation normally occurs in four forms rain sleet snow and hate these forms of precipitation not fall in each and every part of the world let's find out which forms are common in the area where you live so the first one is rain drops of liquid water are rain this is the most common form of precipitation rain can form when tiny Cloud droplets combine Cloud droplets can be millions of times smaller than the raindrops so a great number of these droplets must combine to form a raindrop which is large enough and heavy enough to fall to the ground if the droplets fall through the air that is warmer then the water's freezing point they reach the ground as rain rain can also begin as Ice Crystal the crystals melt as they fall through the warm air producing raindrops second form is called sleet it consists of clear and translucent particles of ice sleet started in the form of ice crystals from the clouds if the temperature between the clouds and the ground is warm enough these ice crystals melt partially or totally and become more like raindrops these raindrops when reached the colder layer of the air near the surface refreeze again it falls to the surface in the form of raindrop size ice pellets called Sleek third form is called snow small Flakes and pellets of ice make up snow snowflakes are produced when the temperature in the cloud is cold enough for water Vapors to form ice crystals the type of snow that falls depends on the temperature of the clouds light fluffy snowflakes form when the temperature is very low warmer temperatures produce heavy wet snowflakes snowflakes can form in different shapes third form is called Hague Hill is made up of round chunks of ice called hailstones hailstones form when drops of rain freezes inside a cloud with strong updraft winds if the winds are strong enough they lift the Hailstone through the inside of the cloud a Hailstone may rise and fall many times each time gaining a new icy coat finally when the Hailstone is too heavy for the wind to lift it falls to the ground the largest documented Hailstone waited more than one and a half pounds the total precipitation in a particular place depends on some weather condition too life temperature pressure wind humidity and other factors hope you enjoyed the lesson today please like share and subscribe to   6. @abdenassermessouci6411: More than fourteen centuries ago, the Quran revealed to humanity the mechanism of hail formation approximately 1400 years ago. In the verse: ﴿أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ يُزْجِي سَحَابًا ثُمَّ يُؤَلِّفُ بَيْنَهُ ثُمَّ يَجْعَلُهُ رُكَامًا... وَيُنَزِّلُ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ مِنْ جِبَالٍ فِيهَا مِنْ بَرَدٍ﴾ — which translates to: "Do you not see that Allah drives clouds? Then He brings them together, then He makes them into a mass... And He sends down from the sky, mountains [of clouds] within which is hail" — Islam unveiled this complex meteorological phenomenon to the world centuries before modern science discovered it. Early Muslim scholars and exegetes understood from this verse that clouds gradually accumulate and merge through condensation processes until they reach massive, mountain-like proportions, and that hail forms within these enormous atmospheric masses before falling to the surface.

Modern science remarkably validates this ancient insight. Cumulonimbus clouds are massive vertical atmospheric cells reaching 12–18 kilometers in height in tropical regions, forming through three key stages that align with the verse's description: the updraft stage where moist air masses gather; the mature stage where rising and falling air intertwine to form a towering "mass"; and finally the dissipation stage. In their upper frigid layers (below 0°C), water droplets crystallize around condensation nuclei, forming hailstones that grow through repeated accretion of icy layers until their weight exceeds the updraft velocity, causing them to collapse gravitationally toward Earth. Thus, Islam demonstrated this scientific reality centuries ago, long before modern meteorology confirmed it, making the convergence between classical Islamic interpretation and contemporary atmospheric dynamics a striking example of the potential harmony between religious texts and scientific inquiry.

- What's a Hurricane? | Weather Science | SciShow Kids: Kind: captions Language: en It’s summer where Squeaks and I live, which means we’ve had a lot of thunderstorms lately! Summer thunderstorms usually have rain, thunder, and lightning, and sometimes strong, gusty winds. Sometimes the power even goes out! Good thing Squeaks and I keep a flashlight here just in case! But some areas of the world experience a really extreme kind of storm: a hurricane. Hurricanes are big, powerful storms that have strong winds. And I mean strong— In order to be considered a hurricane, a storm’s winds have to be blowing faster than 119 kilometers an hour. That’s faster than a car zooming down the highway! Sometimes you’ll also hear this kind of storm called a cyclone or a typhoon, but they’re just different names for the same thing. From outer space, many hurricanes look like this: big, swirling storms that have a really cool shape. [Squeaks squeaks] That’s right, Squeaks, this hurricane looks kind of like a circle! The clouds wind around a space in the center of the hurricane, called the eye. It even sort of looks like an eyeball! Now, a hurricane’s shape is pretty cool, but it’s not the only thing that’s special about this kind of storm. Everything has to be just right for a hurricane to form. It’s kind of like a puzzle. All of the pieces have to be there, and have to fit together in exactly the right way, or a hurricane won’t form. The first piece of the hurricane puzzle is warm ocean water. The water has to be at least 27 degrees Celsius, or about as warm as a swimming pool, for a hurricane to form. The warm water causes the air above it to warm up and rise high into the sky The warm air very humid, which means that there’s a lot of water in it. This warm, moist air rises high into the sky from the warm water below. As the air rises into the sky, other air rushes in to take its place. The air that rushes in gets warm and humid, too, and then it rises high into the sky as well. As the air rises, it cools, and clouds start to form—lots of them! High in the sky is also where we find the second thing that’s important for making a hurricane: wind. And not just any wind. The wind that helps make a hurricane has to be calm and steady. It can’t be the kind of wind that blows really hard for a moment or two, then calms down again. If there are sudden gusts like that, the storm can get blown apart, like how a candle gets blown out. But if there’s warm, damp air along with those steady winds, and it stays like that for a few days, the rest of the pieces that make up a hurricane fall into place. The steady winds push the growing storm over the ocean, and the warm water gives the storm energy. It causes more air to rise, more clouds to form, and the winds inside the hurricane to become stronger. In fact, the only place that it’s not very windy is the hurricane’s eye. It’s actually very calm in the eye — even though it’s right in the middle of the storm! As it grows, the hurricane also starts to turn. It spins around, with the eye in the center, kind of like a top. As long as hurricanes have that warm air for fuel, they can keep going … and keep growing! But when they reach land, or pass over cooler water, they lose their warm air, so they start to fall apart. Hurricanes usually leave behind a lot of rain, though. Sometimes the rain can last for days! They also can can push a lot of ocean water onto the shore, which can cause floods. But people can prepare for hurricanes, especially if they know when they’re going to happen. For example, most hurricanes near North America happen between May and November every year, because that’s when the different pieces that make a hurricane fit together the best. And scientists have lots of tools to try and track hurricanes — they use what they know about science to make really good guesses about where they’ll pop up, where they’ll go, how big they’ll be, and how long they’ll last. In places where hurricanes happen a lot, they usually build buildings in special ways so they won’t get damaged by the strong winds or flooding water. And if you live or go on vacation in a place where hurricanes might happen, you can be prepared, too! You can talk to grownups about what you can do if a hurricane happens. That might mean like bringing in things from outside to protect them from strong winds, or it might mean helping to prepare a kit with food and water, flashlights, and a radio. Speaking of flashlights, I know mine could use some new batteries. C’mon, Squeaks, let’s go to the store. Thanks for joining us for this episode of SciShow Kids! If you want to watch more videos with me and Squeaks and you’re watching this on YouTube, you can click the red subscribe button.

  1. @waynedillon2012 [8 likes]: When someone knocks on the homeowner's door and offers them a free roof paid for by their insurance company, people say yes. That raises the cost of insurance for EVERYONE. As insurance companies figure out which roof ages are likely to get that yes from the homeowner, they make their maximum roof age lower than that number. Insurance is a for profit business and the homeowners insurance industry hasn't made a profit in 5 years. 

The legislature can ease this by allowing companies to pay wind and hail roof claims at actual cash value rather than replacement cost, after all, a roof is a wear item like the tires on your car. Additionally, allowing insurers to pay claims reported more than 6 months after the date of loss at ACV would limit the effect of the roof sales people knocking on every door in the neighborhood. 

The DFS is receiving hundreds of fraud complaints from insurance companies, they have chosen not to act upon those complaints.

The people that are being negatively impacted are the people who pay for their own home maintenance, the agents that are losing their contracts with carriers because of unacceptable loss ratios and the people who invest in companies in the insurance sector; those investors are leaving even taking a big loss to get out and that is also driving tougher underwriting standards. 

Homeowners insurance has become a wealth transfer vehicle, taking money from homeowners and transferring it to the trial bar.

- Geomagnetic storms and solar flares, explained | JUST THE FAQS: Kind: captions Language: en Auroras may look stunning from here on Earth, but the geomagnetic storms that cause them could also pose a potential threat to our planet. Here's why. It all starts 93 million miles away with a tremendous burst of energy from the sun. These explosions can be categorized into two events: CMEs or solar flares. A CME, or coronal mass ejection, is a giant cloud of particles hurled into space. This phenomenon will typically take between one to five days to reach the Earth. A solar flare is a giant burst of x-rays and energy, which takes about eight minutes to make it to our planet. These space weather events only impact Earth when the explosion occurs on the side of the sun that is facing our planet. The explosions produce a gust of solar wind, which hurdles through space, temporarily disrupting the outer part of Earth's magnetic field. This causes a geomagnetic storm, also referred to as a solar storm. Particles flowing from the sun get caught up in the Earth's magnetic field, creating a colorful aurora, otherwise known as the northern or southern lights. The particles interact with molecules of atmospheric gases, causing glowing red and green colors in the sky. Usually only seen in far northern and southern regions like Iceland or New Zealand, a solar storm can wreak havoc on earth, but not in the way you think. While the atmosphere protects us from these harmful rays, a large storm can interfere with radio communication, GPS systems and satellites, can corrode oil and gas pipelines and even cause power blackouts. While scientists have observed thousands of solar flares over the years, none have matched the intensity of the 1859 Carrington Event. The largest geomagnetic storm in recorded history, it crippled telegraphs around the world as sparks flew from equipment, shocking their human operators and even starting fires. The solar storm created an aurora so bright that people in the northeastern U.S. reportedly used the storm's glow to read the newspaper at night. In the 21st century, this event would've had a much more severe impact on today's technology-dependent society. Scientists estimate that a solar storm of that magnitude today has the potential to cost nearly $2 trillion, affect up to 40 million people, and cause blackouts lasting between 16 days to two years. Solar activity, like sun flares and CMEs, fluctuates in 11-year cycles, and right now, it's on the rise. Scientists predict this stormy activity will reach its peak in 2025.
