And putting it all together, what does it all mean for understanding HCI's value and importantly the risks that come with investing in this kind of business? >> It is a complex picture, no doubt about it. But looking at it gives you these crucial insights into how policy, global capital, local business, how they all react not just to crisis, but also to the reforms that come after. I mean, we're talking about a massive swing for HDI. They went from big losses just back in 2022 to projecting record profits in 2024 2025. That kind of turnaround is pretty rare and it tells you a lot about the impact of the changes that were made. >> So get ready for I think some genuinely surprising facts and maybe a few aha moments along the way because this isn't just some dry business analysis. No, >> it's a real case study in resilience and adapting, and you really see the powerful ripple effects when major legislation changes things. By the end, you should have a much clearer picture of what makes this market tick and why it matters. >> Let's dive in. >> Okay, so let's start by setting the scene. Florida's property insurance market. To really get why the recent turnaround is such a big deal, we need to understand just how bad things were before, say, 2022. >> Oh, it was bad. really bad. >> It wasn't just struggling, right? It was more like a full-on crisis. >> Absolutely. For eight straight years leading up to 2022, the Florida property and casualty PNC market was just defined by these deep, deep problems. And you know, it wasn't just about the hurricanes, though obviously they didn't help. It was more fundamental, a breakdown in just how the market was functioning. >> A fundamental breakdown. What was at the core of that breakdown? If it wasn't just storms, what was it? Well, connecting it to the bigger picture, the uh the main issue wasn't really the natural disasters themselves. It was the legal environment. It had become frankly deeply problematic. It fostered just excessive litigation. >> Excessive litigation. >> Yeah. Our sources actually call it a claims cottage industry. Can you picture that? >> A claims cottage industry. >> Wow. That's quite a phrase. >> It paints a picture, right? It was like the legal system, maybe unintentionally, had created these really strong financial incentives. It just became super profitable for certain lawyers, certain contractors to file lawsuits, often over really minor stuff or disputed claims >> instead of just resolving the claims efficiently. >> Exactly. It was almost like a machine turnurning out lawsuits. >> So, it became more profitable to sue than for the insurance system to just work as intended, paying legitimate claims. What were the actual consequences for the insurers, for homeowners? >> Well, the consequences were pretty dire and widespread. You had just rampant litigation. You saw an alarming number of insurance companies actually going insolvent, going bust. >> Wow. >> And just continuous underwriting losses across the board. Lots of insurers just couldn't make money in Florida. So, they either pulled out of the state altogether, drastically limited how many policies they'd write, or like I said, went out of business. >> And that instability must have hit homeowners hard. >> Oh, directly. fewer choices for coverage, premiums that were just skyrocketing year after year. And often the coverage itself wasn't as good. It really created this vicious cycle. Rising costs, shrinking availability. It was bad news for everyone. >> Okay. So, a real crisis situation affecting everybody. But then something big happened. This legislative overhaul between 2022 and 2023. >> You said it wasn't just tinkering. >> No, not tinkering at all. This was a truly structural shift. These weren't just minor adjustments. The legislature held special sessions, which tells you how urgent they thought it was. And they passed these sweeping reforms. The whole point was to fundamentally change the state's tort landscape, you know, the whole legal environment around civil claims. They were going right after the roots of that litigation mess. >> Okay, let's unpack that because this sounds like where things really started to turn. >> What were the key reforms? And maybe more importantly, how did they actually work to fix that litigation problem? >> Well, one of the absolute biggest changes was getting rid of something called one-way attorney fees. >> One-way attorney fees. Explain that. >> Okay. So, the old rule was if a homeowner, the plaintiff, sued their insurance company, and won in court, even if they only won a tiny amount more than the insurer had offered, >> the insurance company had to pay all of the plaintiff's legal fees. Not just their own, but the other sides, too. >> Wow. Okay. I can immediately see how that might create some interesting incentives. How did that play out? >> Think about it like this. If you're an attorney and you know your fees are basically guaranteed by the insurer if you win anything, it creates this massive incentive to file lawsuits. You could take on cases with pretty small damages, maybe even claims that were a bit shaky, knowing that if you managed to win even a little bit, your payday was covered by the insurer. >> So, it fueled an explosion of lawsuits. >> Exactly. It turned lots of small disputes that maybe could have been settled into really expensive legal battles. It was just draining capital from the insurers. >> So by repealing that rule for property insurance claims, the legislature basically tried to remove that primary economic driver for excessive litigation kind of rebalance things. >> That makes sense. A huge change to the economics of suing. What else did they do that was really impactful? >> Another really significant one was prohibiting assignment of benefits or AOB. A OB. I've heard that term. How did that work and why was it such a problem? >> Okay, so under the old system, let's say you're a homeowner, you've got roof damage from a storm. A contractor comes knocking, says, "I can fix that." Then they ask you to sign this AOB form. What that did legally was transfer your right to the insurance benefits directly over to the contractor. So the contractor then basically took over the whole claim process. >> Ah, so the homeowner was kind of out of the loop >> often. Yeah. And this became a massive source of inflated claims because once the contractor had that AOB, they had the legal power to sue the insurer directly. >> I see. >> So the incentive for them was to jack up the repair costs, you know, maybe replace the whole roof when only a patch was needed, knowing they could then fight the insurer in court for every penny, often without the homeowner really being involved or even fully understanding what was happening. >> Wow, that sounds ripe for abuse. >> It really was. So, by banning AOBS for new policies starting January 1st, 2023, the goal was to stop those inflated claims and the lawsuits that followed, putting control back with the actual homeowner. >> Okay, so fewer third parties driving up costs, more control for the policy holder. Any other big changes to how claims were handled? >> Yeah, a couple more important ones. They also significantly shortened the deadlines for filing claims. >> How much shorter? >> Pretty drastically. It used to be you had two years to report a brand new claim and three years if it was a supplemental claim like more damage found later from the same storm. >> Okay. >> Now is down to one year for new claims and only 18 months for supplemental ones. >> Why the change? What does that shorter time frame do? >> Well, for the insurers, it helps reduce what they call the long tale of uncertainty. Older claims are just harder to investigate accurately. They cost more to handle and they're more likely to end up in court as memories fade and evidence gets lost. >> Makes sense. >> And for policyh holders, the idea was to encourage faster reporting and faster resolution. Less time for things to drag out and potentially turn into a dispute. >> Okay. And you mentioned one more. >> Yes. Reforms to the prompt pay law. These basically tighten the timelines for insurers. They now have less time to acknowledge a claim, investigate it, and then make a decision. Either pay it or deny it. That sounds like it benefits the policy holder. Setting clearer expectations. >> That was definitely the intention. Improve the process for homeowners by setting stricter standards for the insurers while also again cutting down the time window where claims could just linger and maybe escalate into litigation. >> So summing up these reforms, it really sounds like this wasn't just about hurricane damage. It was laser focused on controlling that human-made risk, that litigationheavy environment. >> Precisely. It was about rewriting the rules of engagement, like you said earlier, >> taking aim at these structural flaws. >> Exactly. The state basically went after the underlying issues that had made the market such a mess for so long. A direct hit on that claims cottage industry. >> Okay, now this is where it gets really, really interesting. Let's talk about the impact. We've hinted at this dramatic turnaround from losses to profits. How quickly did things change and what did the numbers show? >> Oh, the turnaround was dramatic and frankly pretty stunningly fast. The data is really compelling. Remember those eight straight years of underwriting losses for the Florida specialist insurers? >> Yeah, grim picture. >> Well, in 2024, this is excluding the state-run citizens and the big national carriers. Just looking at the Florida focused guys, they reported their first underwriting profit in eight years. >> The first one in eight years. Wow. After all that red ink. >> Yeah, think about that. Eight years bleeding money, then suddenly boom, profitability. >> What was the most surprising part of that speed? And can you give us some specifics? How big was the swing? >> I think the sheer speed was what surprised a lot of people. It really showed just how much that litigation drag was holding things back once that was lifted or at least significantly reduced. >> Things snapped back. >> They really did. So for specifics, that group of Florida specialist insurers, their combined ratio, remember under 100 is profitable was 93.1% in 2024. And they had an underwriting gain of $26.7 million. Okay, compare that to the year before. >> In 2023, they had a $174.4 million loss. >> Wow, that's a huge swing. Nearly $400 million difference. >> It's massive. And their pre-tax operating income, it surged to $492.3 million in 2024. The year before it was basically just break even. These aren't small changes. They are truly seismic shifts in financial performance. >> Those numbers are incredible. So, was it just the reforms that drove this or was something else boosting profitability, too? >> That's a really crucial point. The reforms were absolutely the foundation. They made profitability possible again. But the turnaround was also significantly fueled by substantial rate increases that insurers had pushed through during those crisis years. >> Ah, right. They've been losing money, so they had to raise rates just to try and survive. >> Exactly. They needed to cover those mounting losses. And those rate hikes were significant. Data from the Florida Office of Insurance Regulations shows the average property insurance premium shot up by 34%. >> 34%. >> Yeah. Just between the end of 2022 and early 2025, the average premium hit $3,23. >> That's a huge jump for homeowners. A real hit to the wallet. So, the total amount of premium money coming into the system must have soared, too. >> It absolutely did. Over a similar time frame, the total direct premium written by these insurers climbed almost 40%. Reaching about$ 23 billion dollars. So the why behind this sudden profitability is really a powerful combination. >> Okay, it >> a sharp drop in litigationdriven claims frequency thanks to the reforms, >> right? >> Combined with significantly higher premiums being earned thanks to those earlier rate hikes. Put those two together and you get what our sources are calling exceptional underwriting profitability. Okay, that clarifies it. Reforms fixed the leaks. Rate hikes filled the bucket. But now the bucket is full and the market looks stable and profitable. What happens next? Does money start flowing in? >> Because profit usually attracts competition. >> You nailed it. That's basic economics. And yes, predictably, restoring stability and profitability acts like a magnet for new capital. The Florida Office of Insurance Regulation, the OIR, they've already noted 15 new PNC companies entering the Florida market since the reforms passed as of August 2025. >> 15 new companies already. So, competition is definitely heating up. What does that mean for the existing players like HCI? Those record profits might not last forever if new guys are jumping in. >> Exactly. Right. This is where we see what's called a profitability competition lag. those huge profits in 2024, they're the result of actions taken back in 2022, 2023, >> right? The reforms and rate hikes, >> but the reaction from the market, new companies forming, raising money, starting to compete for policies that takes time to fully play out, we'll see the real impact of that competition building through 2025 and beyond. >> Ah, okay. So, those exceptionally high margins that companies like HCI are enjoying now, they probably have a finite lifespan. >> Yeah. Eventually, more competition means pressure to lower prices, right? >> Almost inevitably, it's a cycle. And this leads to another really important point. While the insurance companies are celebrating being back in the black, there's a growing political risk, and that's the affordability crisis for homeowners. >> Yeah. That 34% average rate hike is staggering. >> Exactly. So, you have this stark contrast. Record industry profits on one side and homeowners struggling with massive premium increases on the other. That's a politically charged situation, >> especially when you see news reports like that Newsweek story about thousands of Hurricane Debbie claims being closed without payment. That adds fuel to the fire, doesn't it? >> It absolutely does. That kind of story combined with the high premiums creates a really politically sensitive environment. And the implication is huge. If affordability becomes a major political flash point, >> which it seems likely to, >> it could easily lead to future regulatory intervention. You could see politicians pushing for things like rate caps or maybe even mandating premium reductions, >> which could potentially undo some of the benefits the insurers gain from the reforms. >> Precisely. It could unwind some of the benefits, creating a whole new set of challenges. It's a really delicate balancing act for regulators and politicians down there. >> That's a critical point. It's not just about market forces. Politics and social pressure can reshape the whole game. Okay, let's shift gears slightly and talk about something huge but often invisible, reinsurance. You mentioned it's a massive cost. Can you explain what it is and why it's so critical, especially for a Florida insurer like HCI? >> Sure. Reinsurance is uh basically insurance for insurance companies. Simple as that. >> Insurance for insurers. Okay. Think of it as their financial backs stop. For a company like HCI, writing policies primarily in Florida, a state hammered by hurricanes, reinsurance is their single largest cost of goods sold. It's how they protect their own finances from a truly devastating hurricane or series of storms that could otherwise just wipe them out. >> So without it, they couldn't really operate in Florida. >> Honestly, no. Not prudently. Anyway, without strong reinsurance, a major hurricane could bankrupt them. It's absolutely essential. >> Got it. So, it's their crucial safety net, but a very expensive one. And I gather the price and availability of this reinsurance isn't stable. It follows some kind of global cycle. >> Exactly. It's dictated by a global market cycle. And recently, that cycle has been on a real roller coaster. It swung from what felt like a really punishing, expensive, hard market to more recently a much more favorable, capital rich environment. And that shift provides a significant tailwind for HCI. It helps their bottom line directly by making their biggest expense potentially lower or at least more predictable. >> Okay, tell me about that punishing hard market. What happened around 2023 that made it so tough for insurers buying reinsurance? >> Well, 2023 was really the peak of it. Some called it the great reset. What happened was after several years of really big catastrophe losses worldwide, not just Florida hurricanes, but wildfires in California, floods in Europe, you name it, right? and generally poor financial returns for the reinsurers themselves. They basically said enough is enough. They needed to get their own houses in order financially. So at the big renewal period on January 1st, 2023, the market went through this historic reset. >> A reset sounds painful for the buyers. What did that mean for companies like ACI trying to buy their protection? >> Oh, it was definitely a painful correction for them. The reinsurers needing to boost their own profits did a few things. They drastically increase prices sometimes 30, 40, 50% or even more for the same coverage. Wow. >> They also raise the attachment points. That's the level of loss where the reinsurance actually starts paying out. Raising that means the primary insurer like HCI has to absorb more of the losses themselves before the reinsurance kicks in. >> So they keep more risk. >> Exactly. And on top of that, reinsurers tightened up the terms and conditions of the coverage significantly. So insurers like HCI were forced to pay a lot more for protection that often started higher up and might have had more restrictions. It was tough. >> Okay. A much harder, more expensive environment. But then you said things shifted again in 2024, 2025. >> Yeah. >> What turned it around? Why did it become more favorable? >> Well, ironically, it was the success of that hard market for the reinsurers because prices went up so much. They finally started making good money again in 2023 and 2024. >> Ah, success breeds capital. >> Precisely. Strong returns attracted a whole lot of new capital back into the global reinsurance market. AON, one of the big brokers, reported that global reinsurance capital hit a new record high around $735 billion by mid2025. >> $735 billion. That's a huge pool of money. >> Massive. And a growing part of that was alternative capital. Things like catastrophe bonds where investors basically bet on whether catastrophes will happen. That part surged to $121 billion. It showed investors were eager to get back into insurance linked investments once the pricing was right. >> So all this new capital flowing in, more players wanting to sell reinsurance. Does that flip it back to being more of a buyer friendly market for HCI? >> Exactly. More capital means more competition among reinsurers. And yes, that shifts the power dynamic. While the reinsurers have tried to maintain discipline on the terms and attachment points, they haven't forgotten the pain. The pricing has definitely softened. We saw property catastrophe programs that didn't have major losses getting rate reductions maybe 5% to 15% at the January 2025 renewals. Amst even said the balance of power appears to be shifting toward primary carriers. Companies like HCI have a bit more leverage now. >> Okay. So these are direct tailwinds for HCI. They can buy that essential catastrophe protection at more stable and predictable costs. >> That flows right to their bottom line improving their net combined ratio and earnings. >> Absolutely. Lower reinsurance costs are a direct benefit. And we saw HCI successfully complete its big reinsurance program for 2025 2026 back in June 2025, reflecting this calmer, more competitive market that's a significant positive for their financial outlook. >> But I sense there might be a butt coming. Is there a downside to this softening reinsurance market? Does it create other issues? >> There absolutely is a double-edged sword here. And this is a crucial point. While cheaper reinsurance is great for HCI's costs, it also lowers what was probably the single biggest barrier to entry for new insurance companies wanting to start up or expand in Florida. >> Ah, if reinsurance is easier and cheaper to get, it's easier for competitors to jump in. >> Exactly. It significantly reduces the financial hurdle. So this softening reinsurance market will likely accelerate the influx of competition into Florida, which in turn could shorten the duration of the supernormal profits that companies like HCI are enjoying right now. >> So again, we see that cycle good news on costs might mean tougher competition down the road. Yeah, it really shows how interconnected everything is in this insurance world. >> It really does. Market dynamics are always shifting. >> Okay, let's zoom in now on HCI Group itself. We understand the market, the reinsurance cycles. >> How does HCI fit into all this? What's their core business model and strategy? >> Well, HCI is technically a diversified holding company, but let's be clear. Its financial results are overwhelmingly driven by the insurance side of the house. >> Got it. Insurance is king. >> Definitely. Their strategy is deeply rooted in Florida. They try to leverage their own technology and they're known for being pretty opportunistic, capitalizing on market disruptions like other companies pulling back, for example. Essentially, they aim to grow by being smart and efficient in what everyone knows is a really volatile place to do business. >> So, not just passively riding the waves, but actively trying to navigate them using tech and strategy. You said it's a holding company. What are the key pieces under that HCI umbrella? >> Sure. So, first you have the original carrier, Homeowners Choice Property and Casualty Insurance Company, or HCPCI, founded way back in 2007. This is their core focusing on homeowners, condo insurance, renters insurance mainly in Florida. That's the foundation. >> Okay. The traditional base. What about newer stuff? Especially that tech focus you mentioned. >> Right. Then there's Tiptap Insurance Company. They launched Tiptap in 2016. This is positioned as HCI's technology focused insurer. It's been a big driver of their organic growth. Tiptap uses more modern tech for underwriting, for policy administration. The goal is efficiency, speed, precision. often allowing customers to get quotes and buy policies completely online. >> So, a traditional carrier and a more modern techdriven one. Where does the tech itself come from? Is it bought off the shelf or >> No. And this is key. It comes from Exio. This is HCI's own in-house technology division. Exio develops the proprietary software platforms that both HCPCI and Tiptap use. >> Like what kind of software? We're talking about things like their harmony system, which handles all the policy administration, issuing policies, billing, endorsements, everything. And their claim colony platform for managing claims. Having this tech developed in-house is a pretty significant differentiator compared to smaller players who might rely on third party systems. Gives them more control, more customization. >> That sounds like a potentially powerful advantage. Do they have anything else in the group besides insurance and tech? Any diversification? >> Yeah, they do have some. There's Green Leaf Capital, which is their real estate subsidiary. Green Leaf owns and manages commercial properties in Florida, office buildings, retail spots, even some marinas. So that provides a bit of diversification away from pure insurance risk. >> And one more important piece, CL Insurance Company. This is a reinsurance company they set up doiciled in Bermuda. >> Ah, so they're playing the reinsurance game themselves >> in a way. Yes. CL allows HCI to basically reinsure a portion of its own risk. It gives them more flexibility, lets them keep some of that reinsurance premium within the group and helps them actively manage their exposure to the ups and downs of that global reinsurance cycle we talked about. >> Okay, so quite an ecosystem they've built there. Insurance, tech, real estate, even their own reinsur. What's been a primary way they've actually grown their insurance business in Florida, especially with all the market turmoil. A really key part of their growth strategy, especially recently, has been actively participating in Florida's citizens depopulation program. >> Citizens. >> That's the state-run insurer of last resort. Right. The place people go when they can't get private insurance. >> Exactly. And Florida has been trying for years to shrink citizens to get policies back into the private market. The depopulation program is how they do that. Private insurers can apply to take out policies from citizens. >> Okay. So, it's a way to move policies off the state's books and onto private companies. How does HCI approach that? Do they just take any policy? >> No. And this is where their tech comes in again. HCI uses its systems, presumably drawing on lots of data, to analyze the huge portfolio of policies within Citizens. They then selectively choose policies that fit their specific underwriting appetite. Maybe policies in areas with lower historical losses or homes built to better standards. >> So, they cherrypick. Essentially, >> you could say that it's proven to be a very effective way for them to acquire a lot of premium relatively quickly and cheaply, especially as the state is actively encouraging this depopulation. >> Any examples of the scale? >> Yeah, for instance, in the first quarter of 2024, HCI took on about 9,800 policies from citizens. That represented nearly $88 million in annualized premium right there. >> Wow, that's significant growth in one go. And later in 2024, three of their carriers, HCPCI, Tiptap, and another one called Tail Row, were approved to potentially take out up to 25,000 more policies each. So, it allows them to scale up rapidly, but in a controlled, selective way. >> That really highlights the power of that tech for efficient growth. You keep mentioning the technology Exo. How big a competitive differentiator is that really compared to other local Florida >> It's potentially a major factor. HCI has clearly made a significant ongoing investment in building its own tech through Exio. Management argues pretty strongly that these platforms give them real advantages. >> Like what specifically? >> More precise underwriting. They claim being able to assess risk better, price it more accurately, greater operational efficiency, meaning lower costs to run the business dayto-day, and a better claims handling process, which is huge for both cost control and keeping customers happy. In a high volume, high-risisk market like Florida, especially with new competitors coming in, those efficiencies can be critical. >> And there's talk about Exo potentially being spun off. What's that about and why is it considered a big deal? >> Yes, this is something the investment community is watching very closely. HCI management has openly seated their intention to eventually establish Exio as its own separate independent publicly traded company. Maybe through a spin-off to existing HCI shareholders or maybe through an IPO. >> Why would they do that? What's the benefit? >> Well, there are a few potential big benefits. First, it could unlock value. The market might value XO much more highly as a pure play insert techch company than it does when it's just buried inside an insurance holding company. >> Tech companies often get higher valuation multiples. Second, a spin-off or IPO would likely bring in a significant chunk of cash either for Exo itself to grow or for the parent company HCI. And third, it creates strategic clarity. You'd have the insurance business and you'd have the tech business clearly separate that might appeal to different types of investors and make each business easier to understand and value. >> Okay, that makes sense. A potentially significant move that could really change the perception and valuation of the whole group. Mhm. >> Now shifting to the people running the show management and governance. Do they have you know skin in the game? Are their interests aligned with shareholders? >> They definitely do and this is often seen as a strong positive by investors. HCI has substantial insider ownership. The chairman and CEO Pares Patel beneficially owns about 14.4% of the company's stock. >> 14.4% is a lot for a CEO. >> It is. And the executive officers and directors as a group own nearly 22%. That's a significant chunk showing strong alignment. Their fortunes are very much tied to the company's success. >> That certainly suggests a long-term focus. >> Absolutely. And adding to that, the CEO has publicly stated he's never sold any of his shares since the company started. That reinforces a commitment to building long-term value, not just hitting short-term targets. Plus, the board and management team have deep experience insurance, tech, real estate, and many have been with HCI for a long time, providing that stability and knowhow. Okay, one more piece on the company structure. Financial strength ratings. This seems like a point of difference for HCI. They use Demotech, not AM Best, which most people think of as the gold standard. What's the story there? >> Yeah, this is a really important strategic choice common among Florida specialists. HCI's insurance companies HCPCI and TipTap have an A exceptional financial stability rating from Demotech, Inc. >> And that Demotech rating is crucial for >> it's crucial because mortgage lenders in Florida accept it. If your insured doesn't have a rating accepted by Fanny May and Freddy Mack, homeowners basically can't get mortgages on the properties insured by that company. So having that demotech A is essential just to operate in the state. >> Okay, so it meets the minimum requirement. But AM Best is generally seen as tougher, more prestigious, especially by national players. Why not go for an AM Best rating? What's the strategic reason? >> It's a calculated decision. AMBS typically imposes more stringent capital requirements. They generally want insurers to hold more capital relative to the risks they take on. >> More capital means less risk but maybe lower returns. >> Exactly. By not pursuing an AM best rating, HCI can operate with a more flexible capital structure. They might hold less capital than AMBS would require for a similar rating level. And holding less capital, all else being equal, can lead to a higher return on equity, especially during profitable times like now, because you're generating profits on a smaller capital base. >> So, it's a trade-off. Optimized for capital efficiency and potentially higher returns, but maybe you're perceived as having a slightly lower quality rating than the Amst. >> That's a fair way to put it. It creates what analysts sometimes call a quality gap compared to the big national carriers rated by AM Best. And the risk is if there's a really massive catastrophe, something that shakes confidence in the Florida market or stresses multiple demotech rated companies, >> you could see a flight to quality. Consumers, agents, maybe even lenders might suddenly prefer insurers with that top tier AM best rating. In that scenario, HCI could face a competitive disadvantage even with its A from Demotech. It's a strategic choice balancing capital efficiency against perceived financial strength. >> That's a really interesting nuance. Highlights the strategic calculation involved in running an insurance company in such a unique market. Okay, let's dive into the numbers now. The financial performance. We've talked about this incredible turnaround, but let's see it in the data. It really is a tale of two distinct periods, isn't it? Before the reforms and after. >> Absolutely. The financials starkly show the impact of that external Florida market environment on HCI's bottom line. Let's start with the struggle. The pre-reform years, say 2018 through 2022. This period was just marked by steadily worsening profitability. The company was fighting against that tide of litigation abuse we talked about. >> How bad did it get for HCI specifically? What do the key metrics show? >> It got pretty rough. Their combined ratio, that key profitability measure, lower is better, under 100 means profit, it just kept climbing. It hit an unprofitable 122.5% in 2022. So for every dollar they took in as premium, they were paying out over $122 in claims and expenses. >> Exactly. Bleeding money on the core insurance operation. And that resulted in a net loss for the year of $54.6 million. That works out to a loss of $6.24 per share. >> Those are serious losses. And looking at the data table we have, gross premiums written actually peaked then at $775 million in 2022. But the loss ratio, just the claims part soared over 80%. Yeah, writing more business just meant losing more money faster in that environment. >> And return on equity created to negative 24.4%. Book value per share, which measures the company's net worth per share, dropped sharply from nearly $32 in 2021 down to just $18.75 in 2022. That really paints a picture of a company under severe financial strain. >> It absolutely does. A very tough period. But then the reforms hit and the turnaround financially was immediate. and frankly profound. In 2023, just one year later, the combined ratio improved by more than 35 points. >> 35 points down to what? >> Down to a very profitable 87.3%. And that huge swing drove net income to $89.3 million. A complete reversal from that $54 million loss the year before. >> That is an incredible one-year swing. She was the immediate power of those reforms. Did that momentum continue into 2024? >> It absolutely did. And this is maybe even more impressive. In 2024, remember, Florida got hit by three hurricanes that made landfall. Debbie, Helen, and Milton. >> Right. A busy season. >> Even with those storm losses, HCI still managed to improve its combined ratio further down to 83.1%. And that income grew again to $127.6 million. >> So profitable. Even with three hurricanes hitting their main market. That really speaks volumes about the changed environment, doesn't it? The underlying business must be much stronger. >> Exactly. It demonstrates that the legislative fixes created a more resilient model, one that could absorb typical catastrophe losses and still generate significant profit thanks to that better claims environment and the higher premium levels. >> Looking at the 2024 numbers again, gross premiums written jumped way up to nearly $1.2 billion. The loss ratio improved dramatically to 55%. Expense ratio also came down. Eluted EPS hit $8.89. Book value recovered strongly to $4341 and that return on equity went from deeply negative to a very impressive positive 32.5%. Just a remarkable recovery across the board. >> Yeah, a return to really robust financial health. >> And this momentum just kept building into 2025. I understand it sounds like the first half of this year was even better. >> That's right. With no major hurricanes hitting Florida in the first 6 months of 2025, the underlying profitability really shown through. In Q1, net income was $57 million and the combined ratio was an excellent 66.9%. >> Under 70% is fantastic. >> It really is. And then Q2 was even better. Net income of 66.2 million and the combined ratio improved further to an astonishingly low 62.0%. Their gross loss ratio for that quarter was just 21.3%. >> 21% loss ratio. That's incredibly low. It screams claims frequency way down. It's a direct reflection of those reforms working, reducing the non-catastrophe claims noise significantly. And as you'd expect, this accelerating profit is having a huge impact on their equity base. >> How so? >> Well, book value per share jumped from $4341 at the end of 2024 to $5855 by the end of Q2 2025. >> Over $15 increase in just 6 months. >> Yep. Over 34% growth in book value in half a year. That shows rapid value creation and the company getting financially much stronger very quickly. >> That's incredibly fast growth in shareholder equity. So with all this cash flow and profit rolling in, how has management been handling it? What have they done with capital management? >> They've been pretty active and strategic. A big move in 2025 was redeeming all their remaining convertible senior notes. >> Getting rid of debt. >> Exactly. That significantly deleveraged the balance sheet. Their debt to capital ratio dropped below 10% and it cut their quarterly interest payments by more than twothirds. So cleaning up the balance sheet, reducing risk. >> That makes sense. Strengthen the company while times are good. What about their cash position? Their liquidity >> also looking strong. At the end of Q2 2025, the holding company had over $250 million in liquidity. that gives them a really solid cushion and lots of flexibility for future opportunities or unexpected hits. >> And despite focusing on strengthening the balance sheet, they still managed to return capital to shareholders. That's often a tricky balance. >> They have, which points to a disciplined approach. HCI has a long track record here. They've paid a dividend for 46 straight quarters. That's consistency. >> 11 and a half years of dividends. >> Yeah. Since they started, they've returned over $430 million to shareholders through dividends and also share buybacks. and they're still paying $1.40 quarterly dividend per share. So, they're rewarding shareholders while also building that financial foundation. >> Okay, we've seen the impressive turnaround, the strong financials. Now, we have to look at the other side, the risks, because even with all this good news, Florida insurance is inherently risky. What's the biggest most obvious risk for HCI? >> Yeah, you can't ignore the risks. And the biggest one hands down is catastrophe risk combined with their geographic concentration. all their eggs in the Florida basket. Basically, >> pretty much their business is almost entirely in Florida, a state that just gets hit by hurricanes repeatedly. A single massive storm, a hurricane Andrew or Irma type event, or even just a string of smaller but damaging storms hitting populated areas in one season that could potentially blow through their reinsurance protection. >> You see the limits. >> Exactly. And if that happens, it could severely damage their capital base, their earnings, maybe even their solveny. It's the fundamental risk they live with every day. >> And even with sophisticated computer models for hurricanes, those models aren't perfect, right? Nature can always throw a curveball. >> Precisely. Models are not infallible. They buy reinsurance based on their modeled probable maximum loss or PML for say a one in a 100redyear storm. But storms can be stronger, take unexpected paths, stall over cities. Losses can always exceed what the models predicted. So that outsized catastrophe event is always the elephant in the room. What about that regulatory and political risk we touched on earlier? The tension between profits and premiums. >> That risk is very real and probably growing. And that would directly squeeze HCI's margins by increasing their biggest cost. They do try to manage the counterparty risk, the risk a reinsurer won't be able to pay by spreading their coverage across many different highly rated reinsurers. Is this amazing profitability the new normal or just a peak? >> That is absolutely the core debate. And the honest assessment suggests that while the reforms definitely created a much healthier baseline, the kind of profitability we saw in 2024 and early 2025, that's likely a cyclical peak, it probably won't stay that good forever. >> So the key going forward is >> the key is whether HCI's own strengths, particularly its technology, can help it maintain strong underwriting discipline even as competition heats up and inevitably pushes prices down marketwide. Can they build a truly durable competitive advantage or were they just benefiting most from the initial post-reform gold rush? >> So, it's about their internal moat versus the external tide turning again. How have they handled the other big risks like storms and reinsurance? >> Well, so far so good really. They navigated the recent hurricanes effectively from a financial standpoint. They secured their latest reinsurance program in a better market. They've cut their debt way down. These are all positive steps in managing risk. But >> there's always a bud in Florida insurance. The primary unmititigated risk is still that massive hurricane, the one that swamps the models and the reinsurance. That unpredictable natural disaster risk never goes away. >> And the regulatory risk, that affordability tension still lurking. >> You're lurking absolutely. Near-term things like stable regulators probably want the reforms to settle, but medium to long-term, that tension between highinsure profits and painful homeowner premiums is a political wild card. It could definitely lead to unfavorable rule changes down the line if affordability stays a major issue. >> Okay, so wrapping it up, the whole discussion around HCI really boils down to the duration of this favorable period. How long can these good times last? And are HCI's internal strengths, their tech, their management, their capital strategy strong enough to build a lasting advantage that can ride out the inevitable return of tougher competition and market cycles? It's a really finely balanced picture. >> Exactly. What could drive the valuation higher? Continued strong underwriting, especially through a tough storm season. A successful Exo spin-off that unlocks value. Proof they can defend market share profitably. What could drive it lower? A huge hurricane loss, faster than expected price competition or negative regulatory changes. It's definitely a dynamic situation investors need to watch closely. >> Wow, what a deep dive that was. From an industry on the brink of collapse to record profits, all seen through the story of HCI Group, we've really seen the power of specific laws to reshape a market, the hidden influence of global reinsurance cycles, and how one company uses tech and strategy to navigate such a volatile place. >> It really underscores how policy, economics, and business strategy are all deeply intertwined. You can't understand one without the others, especially in a market like Florida. >> So, for you, our listener, thinking about the key takeaways, remember just how potent legislative change can be. Remember the cycles, both local market cycles and those global reinsurance waves. Think about how unique company assets like tech and experienced leaders who have skin in the game can make a real difference. >> And crucially, never forget the risks that persist. That geographic concentration, the affordability tension, and the everpresent threat of that next big storm. They're always part of the Florida insurance equation. >> Absolutely. So, as you mull over all these complex dynamics, here's a final thought, something to chew on. Given how cyclical we know the Florida market and the global reinsurance business are, how do you really tell the difference between a temporary peak in profits, like maybe what we're seeing now, and a truly fundamental lasting shift in how the industry works? What evidence would you need to see to be convinced that this current stability is built to last and not just the quiet before the inevitable next storm? It's a tough question, but central to understanding any market prone to booms and busts.

  15. @geoffbox4455: The anodes work by corroding more easily than the other metals in the water. When any metal (including the anode) corrodes it emits electrons (like electrical current). As the anode corrodes first it "fills up" the other metals with electrons (if they are connected or wired to it). When a metal is "full" of electrons it can't produce any themselves so therefore can't corrode. Metals can only corrode if they are allowed to emit electrons.
Loving the videos. Well done!
  16. @brownnoise357: Anodes got a galvanic isolator? Galvanic transformers (about 600 quid by Victron) are supposed to ve much better. Now something Cheap very cheap in Comparison, which "May" help to extend Expensive Anode Life, potentially Considerably (to think about for Next Haulout), I came across on a couple of Forums where people were talking about what to use for antifouling propellers - Cold Galvanising Zinc Paint. You can get it in Aerosols and tins for brush and roller on, and users of it have had no problems for in excess of 10 years. 96% zinc content in a silicon medium that doesn't even need stirring, 1kg tins in UK, costs about 24 quid Inc VAT. You can Get Marine Zinc Paint from Chandlers but it is about 4 times the price for identical Paint. Aerosols have less Zinc in them, and cost about 10 quid inc VAT 6 dollars ish USA.  3Coats protects for up to 18 months, and it is non Toxic, plus is an Anode (wearing away the surface electrically may be how it works as Antifoul ?  Well I haven't had a chance to try this yet myself due to no boat presently. What I plan to do, as my next boat will have a bolted on  iron Keel , is 2 coats of Epoxy Barrier  Coat then 5 coats of Cold Galvanising Zinc Paint, then 2 coats of rolled on 96% topped with 1 coat of aerosol on the Prop, and 3 coats of 96% on the Fudder. That will be a heck of a lot of Anode Protection all around the Boat, and as I'll be hauling out annually for thorough inspection, Full Service etc, I'll be able to see how the Cold Galvanising is coping, plus see what state the Anodes are in. to refresh the Paint if needed, just pressure wash, give a good scrub and slap more Cold Gal arising on. If your anchor Galvanising looks a bit thin, slap some on that as well. It may be a long wait for an update, but I'll try to remember to give one, with  lose up photos of the paint plus Anode surfaces. Did you get a PVC or Hypalon Dinghy by the way ? If PVC something to extend its life would be a full di ghy cover, lots cheaper than Chaos, and for tubes etc when the cover is off due to dinghy being in the Sun being used ? Try a Spray and wipe Iver with sensitive Skin High Factor Sun Blocker. The Ambre Solaire Factor 50 was 5 quid from Tesco, and this summer Test pieces of PVC showed no degradation. Non Sensitive Skin Protection from UV may be as good, but cost the same so I started with that. There is a new PVC that is much more resistant to UV that Seago use on their PVC inflatables, but as they arevthe same weight as their 3 metre Hypalon  air deck and air keel, I think I'll go for that and wait on more Info on how good the new PVC ones are doing.   17. @nigelpickering9065: Those little weeping spots you found as you scraped out the car filler could be evidence of osmosis I’m afraid. I had the same on my boat and as each little spot was found it was opened to find an acetic liquid oozing out. This was washed out with water before drying and  refilling the holes with epoxy. It wasn’t a permanent fix as to do this really needs the gel coat to be planed and a proper treatment undertaken. Expensive and time consuming. On the plus side I have not heard of a boat sinking from osmosis. 👍

  Top Comments:
  1. @giajia [223 likes]: To summarize:
1. Setting clear objectives and expectations for employers and yourself. What are your employers looking for in a successful intern? Don’t just assume.
2. Check in weekly with your supervisor.
3. Ask questions! Try to solve the problem yourself at first, but ask questions if you get stuck.
4. Set up coffee chats with the people on your team.
5. Participate in company events! Professional, social, workshops, etc.
6. Set daily, weekly, and overall summer goals. Have a to-do list for your summer. (time will fly, own your time!)
7. Be open to criticism, show a growth mindset. 
8. Show your enthusiasm! Positive mindset! 
9. Identify what you don’t like about the internship. 
10. Aim to get a return offer! 

+1 I would also add researching the people you’re working with so that you are well-versed in who’s who. Being prepared beforehand makes networking so much easier.

- How Do Pilots Pass The Time On Long Flights?: Kind: captions Language: en For most passengers, long-haul flights are&nbsp; a matter of endurance aided by a few drinks,&nbsp;&nbsp; inflight entertainment, and trying to get some&nbsp; sleep between meal services. It is an entirely&nbsp;&nbsp; different matter for the people flying the plane&nbsp; as there are still plenty of things to do even&nbsp;&nbsp; after takeoff. Let’s look at what’s happening&nbsp; on the flight deck during these longer flights… Even though flight paths are decided upon&nbsp; before departure, pilots have to monitor&nbsp;&nbsp; weather conditions and decide whether&nbsp; or not to alter them during the flight.&nbsp;&nbsp; On long-haul flights, weather conditions are&nbsp; more likely to change from initial forecasts,&nbsp;&nbsp; with aircraft often encountering multiple&nbsp; weather systems. These systems can vary in&nbsp;&nbsp; intensity from slight turbulence to dangerous&nbsp; thunderstorms. Traveling at high speeds,&nbsp;&nbsp; pilots must be prepared at all times&nbsp; to alter the flight path if necessary. If, for example, they see a&nbsp; thunderstorm on the radar,&nbsp;&nbsp; they must first get the OK from air traffic&nbsp; control (ATC) to change their route. Clear air turbulence is another worry, and&nbsp; because it cannot be seen on the radar,&nbsp;&nbsp; pilots rely upon the information provided by&nbsp; pilots flying the same route ahead of them. When&nbsp;&nbsp; flying across oceans such as the Atlantic, one&nbsp; pilot's job is to communicate with the ATC while&nbsp;&nbsp; the other monitors a common air-to-air frequency&nbsp; that pilots use to communicate with each other. The entire time a flight is&nbsp; in the air, the pilots monitor&nbsp;&nbsp; all kinds of gauges to ensure that the&nbsp; plane performs how it is supposed to. Making sure that the temperature of the fuel&nbsp; is not too cold is another thing pilots must&nbsp;&nbsp; monitor. If the fuel cools down too much,&nbsp; its flow can be restricted due to waxing.&nbsp;&nbsp; If this happens, pilots can rectify&nbsp; the situation by descending, or by&nbsp;&nbsp; increasing the aircraft's speed (and,&nbsp; thus, its skin temperature). However,&nbsp;&nbsp; this isn't a particularly frequent occurrence,&nbsp; as engine oil helps fuel to retain heat. While one of the pilots monitors all the&nbsp; gauges, the other will be busy taking care&nbsp;&nbsp; of the paperwork. Before the flight, the&nbsp; pilots are given a written flight plan.&nbsp;&nbsp; Anything that deviates from this&nbsp; must be written down as notes. Outside of these important activities,&nbsp; long-haul flying can be very boring for pilots.&nbsp;&nbsp; During cruise phases, pilots will often pass&nbsp; the time by making small talk with one another,&nbsp;&nbsp; or doing some reading. Smartphone games and&nbsp; good old fashioned crossword puzzles might be&nbsp;&nbsp; additional forms of entertainment&nbsp; found on the flight deck. Finally, just like passengers,&nbsp; enjoying inflight meals&nbsp;&nbsp; is another part of the long-haul pilot experience. The pilots typically wait until the passengers&nbsp; have been served and then eat separately.&nbsp;&nbsp; They do this so that there is&nbsp; always one person at the controls. For long flights over twelve hours, there&nbsp; are one or two relief pilots onboard&nbsp;&nbsp; so that the two pilots who performed the&nbsp; takeoff can take a break and get some rest. There are just two pilots on shorter&nbsp; long-haul flights of eight or nine&nbsp;&nbsp; hours. The only opportunity they get to&nbsp; leave their seats and stretch their legs&nbsp;&nbsp; is when they go to the bathroom. Did you know about these long-haul duties and&nbsp; activities? Let us know by leaving a comment.

  16. @ankurkulkarni904: Perfect defination of both your oldest kids. An Elven-maid there was of old,
A shining star by day:
Her mantle white was hemmed with gold,
Her shoes of silver-grey.

A star was bound upon her brows,
A light was on her hair
As sun upon the golden boughs
In Lórien the fair.

Her hair was long, her limbs were white,
And fair she was and free;
And in the wind she went as light
As leaf of linden-tree.

Beside the falls of Nimrodel,
By water clear and cool,
Her voice as falling silver fell
Into the shining pool.

Where now she wanders none can tell,
In sunlight or in shade;
For lost of yore was Nimrodel
And in the mountains strayed.

The elven-ship in haven grey
Beneath the mountain-lee
Awaited her for many a day
Beside the roaring sea.

A wind by night in Northern lands
Arose,and loud it cried,
And drove the ship from elven-strands
Across the streaming tide.

When dawn came dim the land was lost,
The mountains sinking grey 
Beyond the heaving waves that tossed
Their plumes of blinding spray.

Amroth beheld the fading shore
Now low beyond the swell,
And cursed the faithless ship that bore
Him far from Nimrodel.

Of old he was an Elven-king,
A lord of tree and glen,
When golden were the boughs in spring
In fair Lothlórien.

From helm to sea they saw him leap,
As arrow from the string,
And dive into the water deep,
As mew upon the wing.

The wind was in his flowing hair,
The foam about him shone;
Afar they saw him strong and fair
Go riding like a swan.

- Hurricane Survival Story: Why Hurricane Preparedness Matters: Kind: captions Language: en In the fall of 2012, Hurricane Sandy formed, ravaged the Caribbean, and powered through the Mid-Atlantic, bringing high wind, coastal flooding, and even snow at higher elevations. Sandy continued north, merged with another storm caused by the polar jetream, then pounded ashore on October 29th near Atlantic City as a rare supertorrm. The storm combined with a full moon and high tide to amplify record-breaking storm surge and drove seawater into lower Manhattan. Roads, tunnels, and subway lines flooded. Power went out and residents were left trapped and in the dark. Along the Jersey Shore, trees toppled onto power lines. Fastmoving water flooded streets and crashing waves destroyed homes that had survived foul weather for decades. The storm shredded iconic landmarks like the Seaside Heights boardwalk leaving shell shocked residents and a debelined beach behind. With more than 68 billion in damages and 286 lives lost in seven countries, Sandy had left her mark. Look at this. They had to break it up to get it out of the street. All of these houses on this street are gone but two. In Union Beach, New Jersey, the Sohaki family was unaware that their home would be no match for the powerful storm because the 1940s bungalow had survived nor easterers for nearly 75 years. But as Sandy approached, they started to realize that this one would be different. I wanted to prepare because the warnings were stronger than any other storm we'd had. This one seemed a lot different and I just wanted to get some things over next door to my mom's house where it would be safe. Between the two families, there are 11 animals. Leaving them behind to evacuate would probably not be something that could be easily dealt with. Around 4:00 in the afternoon, with the wind picking up and my concern about the wind against the structure itself, I suggested that it maybe be best that we move the animals and ourselves next door to my mother-in-law's reinforced concrete house. 7 years before, Seth and Karen helped her mom build a new home next door to their bungalow. They built it with concrete because concrete is energy efficient and lowmaintenance. It was simple to erect. You stack these blocks up like Legos. You put in reinforcing bars as you stack them. And then once the wall height is reached, you have your wall shape. You pour concrete in, it sets up, and you're finished with a insulated strong concrete wall. The concrete home that he built for his mother-in-law was a three-story home built to current coastal construction standards. By building with insulating concrete forms, he was getting high R value from the insulation that's in the wall assembly. What he was getting in addition to that was the high wind resistance that comes with reinforced concrete. It's really the strongest material you can use for residential construction. Seth and Karen knew her mother's new home would save money on energy and maintenance, but they never imagined it would mean the difference between life and death by saving them from the storm. Over the course of the evening, the Sohackis watched from the concrete home as their beloved bungalow next door was torn off its foundation by the violent waves. The waves were getting taller as it was getting nearer to high tide. The wind was starting to rip pieces of siding off our house. I looked out the window and saw the cars were gone. And then within a half hour, I looked out another window on the other side and realized our house was gone and that all the houses around us were gone. Once the storm passed, the Sohakis emerged from their refuge next door to see what was left of their bungalow, and the only thing remaining was the foundation. Everything else around them was destroyed, except for the concrete home. This town is is destroyed. You knew it just by looking at this one street and just looking glancing inward up one block, you could see the devastation was everywhere. It was as if the world had suddenly decided to pick everything up and just put it somewhere else. Concrete house was fine. The concrete house was standing. It didn't suffer any damage that I could see other than cosmetic to the exterior and the garage doors which were open. A storm like this that we never thought could happen can indeed happen. Being in this house was the greatest thing that we could have done. And I'm glad that we had this house to come to next door because I felt utterly secure inside this home. Never take things like this lightly. Always be prepared for the worst. For 15 years, we've been sharing the tale of two homes that unfolds in every disaster. One home survives and one does not. Seth and Karen's powerful story of resilience tells the tale and its life-saving lesson yet again. We have choices when building and choosing our homes. And we can have it all. Savings, strength, but most importantly, safety and survival. [Music]

  16. @darthvader5300: Ever heard of Smeaton cement mortar and when gravel is added it is transformed into Smeaton concrete? Mortar is when a cement binder is only mixed with sand. But when one adds gravel to the mortar mix before adding water, then only then it becomes concrete. The Smeaton mortar base at the abandoned foundation is still as good as new AND UNAFFECTED BY SEAWATER AND BY THE COASTAL MARINE CONDITIONS. It was only abandoned because the natural rock foundation below it was being eroded by the Atlantic waves pounding against it for 200 to 300 years. It is a mixture of equal volumes of 1 part slaked lime + 1 part powdered blast furnace iron slag + 1 part clay powder. The powdered blast furnace iron slag is pulverized, crushed, grinded, and milled to be as fine as baby powder or face powder or bleached white flour powder and at that stage of ultrafiness it will dissolve in water just like the slaked lime powder and clay powder.That Smeaton cement binder mixture is then mixed with sand and gravel at a volume of a ratio of 1 part Smeaton cement binder + 2 parts sand + 4 parts gravel. Smeaton cement binder is also a hydraulic cement that hardens and cures underwater and undersea water. At least that Smeaton cement binder has been time-tested and time-proven for 200 to 300 years!

  18. @metekavruk_Alanya: We use only concrete for ages. There are of course bad sides too if you do not be carefull. 

#1 enemy to concrete is water. When two meets together concrete will crack and leak water. Bad isolation of foundation will cause water climb up, specially basement floors will get wet and throw paint and any plaster layer off. You will always have dark green colors on walls and will smell terrible. Water leakage and dump also may occur around window frames even if you sealed them tight. Roof is also very important. Bad roof will bring dump at top floor and ceiling. Plastic sealers must be refreshed when their time comes. You have good external wall isolation which is perfect. 

A concrete house will always move because of it's weight so  your base soil and foundation both must be rigid. There are many luxury houses cracking from middle because of bad foundation, bad reinforcing. But small movements are normal as long as there is no cracks on main critical structure - no panic. Also wall bricks seperation from concrete structure may occur and is normal if happens once, repeating ones mean trouble. In this video i see soil is wet because of sea level so bad construction will definately cause the problems i mentioned above.

You can not drill big holes on pillars as it will cause loose in rigidity and never cut any reinforcement steel bar for renovation. You should not remove a pillar.

Plumbing, wiring etc must be preplanned before pouring the concrete because cutting main structure later randomly may cause troubles. Engineers must design routes and agree with you. Spend time on planning. Wirings will go through plastic pipes buried in concrete or wall bricks so plan it earlier and TAKE A COPY OF ELECTRICITY AND PLUMBING PROJECT.

A concrete structure will age in  time depending on quality and conditions. A bad structure will show defects say in 10 - 20 years ,  a good one will last for 30-60 years. More floors mean more pillars, beams and so more stress on structure. Old buildings may have linear cracks and fall of concrete off in some parts. Humidity also is a reason for this.Experts must check the latest condition and decide on necessary fixes. If steel reinforcements become visible then do not insist on saving the house, just destroy it because it is already dead.

I am a real estate agent - not an engineer so please ask your engineer questions for more accurate info.There are many types of concrete chemical with different characteristics.
  19. @darthvader5300: The styrofoam should had been replaced with aerate AAC concrete foam pre-mixed with wollastonite to make it more ductile and even make it even better than styrofoam which is plastic. And the concrete mix must be pre-mixed with either 33.33 degrees angle double twisted steel fibers that are 33 twistings long or with helix steel fibers to doubly reinforce the concrete together with conventional steel rebars. The weakest spot of any steel fiber reinforced concrete are in the in between liquid pourings because their steel fibers are not intertwined and interlocking continously with each other if the pouring is not continuously monolithic and non-stop until all the molds are filled and fitted with powerful mold vibrators. Especially the weakest spot when the next liquid pouring is poured on an already solidified portion of the steel fibers reinforced concrete that prevents the steel fibers of the solidified steel fiber reinforced concrete and the steel fibers reinforced freshly poured concrete from intertwining and interlocking with each other. This the need for conventional steel rebars reinforcement with conventional rebar spacing modified to accommodate the steel fibers to flow between them and around them with the aid of concrete superplasticizers and powerful aquare shaped concrete probe vibrators and concrete mold vibrators.

How can one remain calm when [music] the greatest power of nature is bearing down on you? For many years, we studied the tribes [music] of the Great Plains, marveled at the builders of longhouses, and were amazed by the resilience of the gatherers of the subarctic forests. They lived in constant anticipation of powerful tropical cyclones, turning deadly winds into a routine and an integral part of their annual cycle. How did they manage not just to survive, but to thrive where modern technology [music] fails? Today, we will uncover the secrets that the mangrove thickets have [music] hidden for centuries. The hidden truth that everyone keeps quiet about completely overturns our understanding of how ancient people related to the most powerful and devastating force on the planet. The Florida Seminoles, who inhabited the vast swampy territories of the southern peninsula, never viewed an approaching tropical storm as a sudden catastrophe or divine punishment. For this incredibly pragmatic people, a powerful hurricane was as natural and inevitable a season as a cold winter on the plains. They learned to coexist with that terrifying natural phenomenon from which modern people prefer to flee immediately to safe distances. This unique people viewed the piercing, howling wind, and the approaching massive waves as an integral part of the eternal natural cycle of renewal of the Florida land. The Seminole culture was entirely devoid of any desire to artificially subdue nature or erect impenetrable walls in the path of massive air masses. They understood perfectly well that any rigid resistance to the incredible pressure of hurricane force winds would inevitably lead to the complete [music] destruction of even the sturdiest wooden structures. Instead of building massive protective shelters, they relied on incredible flexibility, lightness, and the ability to literally blend into the surrounding swamp [music] landscape. Their compact settlements organically dissolved into the dense vegetation, posing [music] absolutely no challenge to the colossal natural force sweeping in from the ocean. Such an architectural approach required the deepest practical knowledge of the laws governing the movement of wind and water across vast open spaces. The traditional hurricane season on the peninsula lasted several long months and demanded constant, almost instinctive readiness from every resident. The Seminoles structured their entire complex annual rhythm >> [music] >> exclusively around the expectation of inevitable strong winds and torrential tropical rains. [music] The corn harvest, the gathering of timber for construction, and preventive repairs to their tall dwellings were always planned with the inevitable arrival of a powerful storm in mind. The daily routine of this vast tribe never came to a complete halt, but rather smoothly transitioned into a new, more sheltered mode of operation. People methodically adapted their customary daily activities to the conditions of constantly rising humidity and winds that grew stronger with each passing day. The specific Florida climate dictated its own strict building codes, requiring constant cross-ventilation [music] of all wooden structures being erected. Dense swamp vegetation and high humidity completely blocked the free movement of fresh air at the very surface of the soft earth. This is [music] precisely why the Seminole intentionally raised the smooth floors of their homes on special [music] tall stilts, creating powerful natural ventilation from below. The empty, ventilated space beneath the wooden flooring ensured that the interior of the house remained dry even during the most severe ocean floods. This ingenious engineering trick allowed them to work and rest comfortably amidst the dense swamp vapors and constant [music] dampness. The southern people's attitude toward accumulated material possessions differed radically from the modern approach we are accustomed to. >> The sudden loss of their old dwelling during a particularly severe and violent storm was never perceived by them as an irreparable loss. All the necessary natural materials for building a completely [music] new, sturdy house were always found in abundance right at their feet. The complete restoration of a structure destroyed by the wind took only a few days of [music] coordinated collective labor by many adult members of a single large community. The primary, indisputable value was considered to be exclusively >> the accumulated knowledge of how to properly tie support posts and skillfully weave palm roofs. It would be very interesting to hear your perspective on this unusual perception of destructive natural phenomena.
