A secret passed down from generation to generation is hidden in the simple atmospheric [music] changes that precede the powerful onslaught of a tropical storm. The Seminole could unerringly detect the relentless approach of a [music] destructive hurricane long before the strong winds began to bend the branches of tall trees. For hours, they would watch intently [music] for the slightest change in the color of the high sky, which took on a very specific leaden [music] green hue before the storm. Tall, feathery clouds resembling endless, [music] elongated threads served as the very first and most reliable warning of a storm brewing over [music] the ocean. These light and transparent bands of cloud moved incredibly quickly across the entire sky, significantly out pacing the main heavy air masses. The adult inhabitants of the settlements paid the closest attention to the physical behavior [music] of the water in the wide bays and countless inland marshes. [music] Several long days before the direct hit of the storm, the level of the dark [music] water began to rise very slowly, but absolutely steadily and surely. This frightening hydrological phenomenon was caused by the hurricane's colossal [music] atmospheric pressure relentlessly pushing vast masses of ocean water deep into [music] the flat mainland. Familiar coastal landmarks and protruding tree roots gradually disappeared beneath the advancing mass of water in the dense mangrove [music] thickets. The current's speed in the narrow channels changed abruptly, unmistakably signaling [music] to people a global disruption of the region's usual water balance. The direction and general nature of the wind were the most important and accurate natural indicators for long-term weather forecasting. The usual gentle sea breeze was gradually replaced by sharp, cold, and gusty currents of air that carried the pungent smell of seaweed washed ashore. This pre-storm wind did not blow constantly, [music] but swept over the camp in short, yet incredibly strong and dense gusts. Experienced members of the tribe could accurately determine the approximate distance to the center of the [music] approaching storm by measuring the intervals between these powerful gusts. The shorter the lulls between the powerful [music] gusts became, the closer the destructive epicenter of the tropical hurricane was to the settlement. Deep into the night, the main harbinger [music] of a severe hurricane was a very unusual, but well-known glow around the moon's disc. A large luminous ring formed [music] in the sky due to a dense cluster of microscopic ice crystals in the uppermost layers of the tropical atmosphere. The brighter and wider this nocturnal ring became, the more rainwater the cyclone approaching the land carried with it. The familiar stars appeared very dim and heavily blurred through the approaching [music] dense gray veil of high, uniform storm clouds. The boundless night sky completely lost [music] its usual depth, quickly turning into a solid, heavy, and oppressive gray dome over the swamps. The temperature [music] of the humid air just before the strongest storm could rise quite sharply, creating the effect of a giant greenhouse over the forest. An incredibly thick and sticky [music] fog rose from the surface of the warm marshes, which did not dissipate [music] for a long time, even in very strong winds. The stifling hot and humid air was evidence of the accumulation of colossal thermal energy in the lower layers of the thickening atmosphere. The massive trees began to secrete much more sticky resin, trying to protect their rough bark from the excessive [music] and destructive humidity. The scent of this incredibly fresh tree resin mingled thickly with [music] the pungent aroma of the vast number of aquatic plants blooming all around. A comprehensive and thoughtful analysis [music] of all these diverse natural signs completely replaced any modern meteorological instruments for the observant [music] Seminoles. They methodically pieced together a complete picture of the approaching tropical [music] storm from the elongated shape of the clouds, the scent of the heavy wind, [music] and the density of the swamp water. Each such observation was invariably confirmed by years of practical experience >> and discussed in detail at serious councils of adult community members. Errors in these complex natural calculations were virtually impossible, as this multi-level forecasting system had been honed over many centuries. The surrounding nature itself provided [music] them with enough time and obvious clues to prepare in time for the major climatic challenge. This accumulated knowledge allowed people to always stay one vital step ahead of the destructive and blind forces of nature. In the next [music] chapter, you will learn what unique architectural ingenuity they employed to build sturdy houses without a single solid wall. Something you've never seen before, a perfect and incredibly sturdy house that completely lacks the usual exterior walls. The traditional wooden dwelling of the Florida Seminole is known simply as a chickee, which translated into modern terms simply means a cozy home. This lightweight structure is radically and completely different from the massive longhouses of the Iroquois or the dense [music] leather teepees of the Great Plains Indians. A classic chickee consists of a perfectly level wooden platform raised high above the damp ground on very sturdy and thick cypress posts. Above this open platform rises a huge massive roof skillfully woven from hundreds of dried broad leaves of a tropical palm tree. The main architectural uniqueness of this chickee lies in the absolute and complete absence of any solid exterior wooden walls. In the harsh conditions of Florida's incredibly humid climate, solid walls would inevitably lead to the immediate formation of destructive mold and stifling stuffiness. The completely open structure allowed the blowing wind to freely and unimpededly ventilate the dwelling from all four sides [music] around the clock. Such constant and strong circulation of fresh air kept the wooden floors completely dry and maximally extended [music] the service life of the entire structure. At the same time, the incredibly wide overhangs of the massive palm leaf roof very reliably protected the interior of the platform from lashing [music] sideways tropical downpours. The specific selection of suitable wood for the load-bearing support [music] piles was strictly regulated and based entirely on the people's rich practical experience. The Seminole used only straight trunks of swamp cypress, which was distinguished by its incredible natural resistance to rapid decay in a warm, humid environment. These thick cypress logs were carefully and meticulously stripped of their rough bark and driven incredibly deep into the soft, pliable swamp soil. These carefully calibrated support posts could stand for many decades in [music] murky, stagnant water, fully retaining their original colossal strength and hardness. It was precisely these posts that always ensured the fundamental unshakable stability of the entire lightweight wooden structure during the most powerful ocean storms. All fasteners in the classic chickee completely and categorically excluded the use of any metal nails or staples familiar to us. Wooden beams, long rafters, and load-bearing piles were firmly joined together solely through the use of highly complex systems of joints and lashings. Long, flexible roots of certain plants, and narrow, sturdy strips of carefully tanned thick leather >> were actively used as reliable ropes. During very strong and sudden gusts of wind, such tight joints never snapped in half, but merely flexed slightly under the enormous load. This incredible flexibility allowed the entire tall house to sway smoothly with the wind, absorbing the colossal destructive kinetic energy of the storm. The interior layout of such a chickee was always extremely minimalist, austere, and strictly governed by functional rules of space saving. High up near the roof, long ropes were stretched taut for drying spare clothing and safely storing light baskets [music] of food supplies. In the very center of the wooden platform, a special open space was often left for a small, constantly smoldering hearth. The acrid smoke from this fire rose unimpeded through small openings near the ridge of the thatched roof. The blazing fire was built exclusively on a very thick layer of sand or clay, which reliably protected the wooden floor from accidental ignition. The traditional construction of a new chickee was always a large-scale collective endeavor, firmly uniting many adults in a single effort. Strong men handled the heavy task of harvesting massive [music] cypress logs in the forest and the precise erection of the building's load-bearing wooden frame. Experienced women took on the incredibly painstaking and time-consuming task of gathering and carefully, tightly laying thousands of palm leaves [music] on the roof. Such organized collective labor made it possible to fully erect a sturdy and [music] reliable house in just a few full days of continuous work. Each new Seminole forest settlement always consisted of a picturesque cluster of these incredibly light, well-designed, and elegant structures. This unique open architecture boldly challenged all conventional notions of solid walls and reliable protection from the elements. Please like this video if you too were amazed by such incredible engineering ingenuity and the ability of ancient people to harness the laws of physics. Here is what happens behind closed doors just before the arrival of a devastating storm, when the heavens are ready to fully unleash their fury [music] upon the earth. Preparations for the first strike of a massive hurricane began immediately after the first undeniable and alarming atmospheric signs appeared in nature. The Seminoles never succumbed to panic. Their well-rehearsed actions were incredibly precise, calibrated, and honed [music] to the point of automaticity through years of training. Every forest settlement [music] instantly shifted into a special state of maximum alertness, where every adult knew exactly what their specific duties were. The time for fuss was irrevocably lost. The most critical period of methodically securing all property and food supplies was rapidly approaching. The greatest asset of the large community, aside from the wooden dwellings themselves, had always been the long transport canoes hidden in the reeds. These sturdy boats provided vital mobility, and their sudden loss would mean complete isolation amidst the deep, flooded mangrove forest. The heavy wooden canoes were urgently hauled far up onto the high bank and tied incredibly securely [music] to the thickest trunks of old cypress trees. To prevent the howling wind from lifting these boats into the air, they were always turned upside down and pressed [music] tightly against the ground. Sometimes empty canoes were deliberately filled with heavy, smooth stones and [music] wet mud to maximize their weight before the hurricane struck. All of the tribe's food supplies required particularly careful, multi-layered protection from the [music] ever-present water and all-pervading tropical humidity. Dried provisions, hard corn, and edible roots were quickly transferred into incredibly tightly woven baskets made of marsh reeds. These precious, full baskets were hung high under the very ridge of the chickee roof, where rising water could not reach [music] them even during a severe flood. Some essential foods were wrapped very tightly in huge, broad leaves and coated with a thick layer of sticky clay to ensure complete airtightness. Such sealed clay cocoons could lie in murky [music] water for weeks on end, keeping the food inside completely dry and edible. In the most protected center of the settlement, they always prepared an incredibly large supply of completely dry firewood [music] to maintain the vital fire after a This firewood was very tightly covered [music] with several layers of broad palm leaves and old, resin-soaked, waterproof [music] leather awnings. Maintaining the ability to start a hot fire incredibly quickly was critically important for subsequently [music] drying wet clothing and preparing hot food. For the same vital purpose, completely dry plant tender and sharp flints were always carefully hidden in special [music] airtight tubes. These precious sealed tubes were constantly carried on their belts by the most experienced [music] and responsible adult members of the Native American tribe. All heavy clothing and thick winter capes made of animal skins were folded as carefully as possible >> into very tight, dense, and compact rolls. These prepared rolls were packed into special, spacious, waterproof [music] bags expertly sewn from carefully treated thick hides. A massive storm was crashing down on the coast as a solid, impenetrable wall of incredibly dense rain and [music] a wildly howling, roaring wind. The familiar horizon instantly and completely vanished behind a thick veil of flying gray water. And the sky merged with the raging, [music] churning swamps. Tall, old trees began to bend submissively toward the ground emitting a very loud crackling sound under the colossal pressure of the heavy air masses. The murky water beneath the chickee platforms rose rapidly turning every dwelling into a tiny wooden island amid a raging freshwater ocean. The longest, darkest, and most incredibly tense part of Florida's annual hurricane season was beginning. The preparations ended in complete silence unconditionally yielding to the primal power of the howling wind and lashing water. In the next chapter, you will learn exactly how they coped with this terrible weather in reality without leaving their open dwellings. The geographical location of the Seminoles' hidden forest settlements played a key and absolutely decisive role in their survival during the most powerful hurricanes. They never built their light, cheap chickees on the open and completely [music] defenseless sandy beaches of Florida's salty ocean coast. Their compact homes were always very securely hidden deep within the intricate labyrinths of dense mangrove forests [music] and endless freshwater swamps. These amazing mangrove trees formed a unique, incredibly dense buffer [music] zone between the raging ocean and all the inland areas of the peninsula. It was this vast, living green wall that always bore the very first, most powerful, and devastating blow of any tropical storm. Mangrove thickets possess an incredibly complex, branched, and powerful root system that rises high above the constant level of dark water. These endlessly intertwined, sturdy stilt roots always form an incredibly dense network capable of effectively dissipating the colossal energy of onrushing ocean waves. When a terrifying storm surge drove a gigantic [music] mass of water straight from the ocean, the mangrove roots acted as a giant, impenetrable, [music] natural filter. The enormous wave passing through this rigid barrier rapidly lost its initial height, speed, and destructive kinetic energy. Only a relatively gentle, though significant, rise in the level of muddy water reached the Native American wooden settlements without the aggressive, destructive [music] surf. The voluminous canopies of these remarkable trees also served an incredibly important and irreplaceable protective function for the entire peninsula. The dense, tough foliage and incredibly tightly intertwined branches created an incredibly powerful barrier [music] in the direct path of the roaring hurricane force winds. The very fact that the settlements were located deep within the center of the flat peninsula significantly reduced the intensity of the passing cyclone. It is well known that massive hurricanes are always fueled by the energy of warm ocean water and making landfall inevitably and rapidly weakens their structure. Moving slowly over the damp, but still solid lands of the Everglades, the terrible storm gradually lost its initial thermal fuel. By the time it reached the remote villages of the reclusive Seminole, the powerful cyclone had often transformed into an ordinary, albeit very strong, lingering storm. The clever Indians masterfully exploited this immutable geographical law of the harsh natural world to their maximum >> and obvious advantage. The mangrove forests also reliably and firmly held back [music] vast masses of soft soil from total erosion and complete washout into the ocean. It was these sturdy trees that kept the shoreline stable and unbreakable [music] throughout many long and turbulent centuries. Experienced Seminoles knew for certain that the soft earth beneath their wooden homes would never [music] disappear even after the fiercest and longest hurricane. This absolute confidence in the incredible stability of the forest soil was the main foundation for the construction [music] of their permanent wooden platforms. The unique ecosystem of the Florida swamps possessed an incredible, astonishing ability to recover very quickly after any storm. Mangrove branches broken by strong winds rotted incredibly quickly in the warm swamp water generously fertilizing the soil for new green shoots. This centuries-old alliance between the Florida Indians and the [music] dense mangrove forests was an example of an incredible and ideal natural symbiosis. The sturdy trees always provided people with reliable protection, excellent building [music] materials, and a safe place to live in seclusion. In return, the people always treated the forest with incredible care never cutting it down unless absolutely necessary for survival. They carefully took from nature exactly as much as was needed to sustain their modest lives and repair their dwellings. Moving on foot around the camp became absolutely impossible. Any activity required the mandatory use of wooden transport canoes. They incredibly carefully cut only the largest, widest, and greenest leaves, carefully leaving the young shoots for the trees' continued growth. These longboats, hollowed out from a single massive cypress trunk, were true masterpieces of the Indians' unrivaled shipbuilding. They had a completely flat bottom and an incredibly shallow draft, allowing them to glide easily over the water's surface, barely touching underwater obstacles. The role of wooden canoes during this incredibly difficult post-storm period grew exponentially, becoming the sole guarantee of survival. The lengthy construction of such a massive canoe was an incredibly complex and labor-intensive process, [music] requiring immense patience and skill from the builders. Experienced Seminoles selected the straightest, most massive cypress trunk, felled it, and incredibly slowly burned out the core using controlled fire. The finished, enormous boat turned out to be incredibly light, astonishingly sturdy, and completely resistant to the inevitable rot in the damp, marshy environment.
